proposals.title: A Multi-Agent Learning Framework for Real-Time Orchestration in Volatile Integrated Satellite-Terrestrial Networks
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of low-earth orbit (LEO) mega-constellations and terrestrial 6G networks creates a highly dynamic integrated architecture. Pre-2026, managing the joint resources of this system is challenged by volatile user densities (e.g., from moving platforms) and fluctuating antenna availability (e.g., due to satellite handovers, reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) blockages, or terminal mobility). Existing optimization frameworks lack the agility to handle this dual volatility at the network scale.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-agent, learning-augmented optimization framework, treating volatile user and antenna populations as intrinsic, time-varying system states, can achieve near-optimal, real-time cross-layer resource orchestration for global utility maximization.
proposals.method: We will formulate the orchestration as a constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP) capturing spatial-temporal resource dynamics. A novel multi-agent hierarchical learning architecture will be designed: a meta-controller at the network core uses large-scale optimization (e.g., distributed ADMM) for slow-timescale inter-layer partitioning, while distributed intelligent agents at satellite gateways and edge nodes employ lightweight, physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) or meta-learning for fast-timescale adaptation to local volatility. The framework will integrate concepts from rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) for robust spectral efficiency under antenna population changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified algorithmic control plane for volatile multi-layer networks, a set of stable training protocols for the hierarchical multi-agent system, and quantitative benchmarks showing resilience and rate gains over static or myopic schemes in simulated LEO-terrestrial scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit joint modeling of dual volatility (users and antennas) as first-class system states and the synergistic fusion of large-scale convex optimization for network slicing with distributed, lightweight AI models for real-time, physics-constrained adaptation.

proposals.title: Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Robust Successive Interference Cancellation in Dynamic LEO Satellite Networks
proposals.motivation: Non-terrestrial networks (NTNs), particularly LEO satellite constellations, are critical for global 6G coverage but face severe challenges: highly dynamic topologies, latency constraints, and hardware imperfections at user terminals. Imperfect Successive Interference Cancellation (SIC), a cornerstone of advanced multiple access schemes like RSMA, drastically degrades spectral efficiency and fairness in these conditions. Current optimization-based methods are too slow to adapt, and conventional machine learning lacks robustness to the wide distribution of unseen SIC impairment models and network states.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) agent, trained over a broad distribution of simulated SIC imperfection models and network dynamics, can learn a robust prior that enables fast online adaptation of transmission and decoding policies, maintaining high sum-rate and fairness in real-world NTN conditions with unknown and time-varying impairments.
proposals.method: We will formulate the joint power allocation and SIC decoding order adaptation as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP), where the hidden state includes the precise SIC imperfection profile. The core agent will be built on a recurrent meta-RL architecture, such as RL^2 or a recurrent PPO variant, to maintain memory of past interactions. It will be meta-trained using a simulator that generates episodes from a parameterized family of SIC error models (e.g., residual interference power as a function of signal strength) and stochastic LEO topology changes. The trained policy will infer the latent impairment parameters from limited sequences of pilot observations and channel state information, then output robust precoding (aligned with RSMA designs) and user ordering.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel meta-RL framework for robust, fast-adaptive resource management in NTNs under hardware imperfections. 2) A comprehensive simulation-based analysis demonstrating significant gains in time-averaged spectral efficiency and fairness over static optimization and conventional deep RL baselines in dynamic LEO scenarios. 3) New insights into the fusion of imperfect physical-layer modeling with agentic AI for future intelligent communications.
proposals.novelty: This work uniquely fuses imperfect SIC physical-layer modeling with meta-RL for fast adaptation in NTNs, moving beyond single-scenario optimization or standard RL that fails under distribution shift. It directly extends the team's work on RSMA precoding and agentic AI into the challenging, dynamic NTN domain with hardware impairments.

proposals.title: Robust Multi-Cell Rate-Splitting Precoding for Dense LEO Constellations Under Stochastic Orbital Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of dense Low Earth Orbit (LEO) mega-constellations promises global broadband coverage but introduces severe, time-varying multi-cell interference and uncertain channel states due to orbital perturbations and satellite mobility. Existing terrestrial robust precoding methods, designed for static cell geometries, fail to account for the macroscopic topological uncertainty inherent in non-geostationary satellite networks, risking significant performance degradation and service outages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-tier robust optimization framework, co-designing Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) precoding with stochastic models of orbital dynamics, can guarantee spectral efficiency and service continuity in dense LEO networks despite both imperfect CSI and constellation topology uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will formulate a multi-cell robust optimization problem where the objective is to maximize a weighted sum-rate under bounded CSI errors (first tier) and probabilistic constraints on satellite visibility and inter-satellite interference derived from stochastic orbital mechanics models (second tier). The problem will be solved using a novel iterative algorithm combining Successive Convex Approximation (SCA) for the non-convex RSMA design with scenario-based or distributionally robust optimization techniques to handle the topological uncertainty, leveraging tools like the Systems Tool Kit (STK) for realistic orbital simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, practically implementable robust precoding and resource allocation framework specifically for dense LEO networks. It will provide rigorous analytical bounds on performance under dual-layer uncertainty and demonstrate significant gains in spectral efficiency and robustness over conventional robust multi-cell MIMO and non-robust RSMA baselines via simulation.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit integration of stochastic orbital dynamics and potential node failures into the robust multi-cell RSMA design problem, moving beyond terrestrial robust optimization which only considers CSI imperfection, thereby enabling communication strategies co-designed with the operational physics of satellite constellations.

proposals.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Joint User Scheduling and Robust Precoding in RSMA Systems with Imperfect CSI
proposals.motivation: Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) is a powerful framework for multiuser MIMO systems, offering superior spectral efficiency and robustness. However, its performance critically depends on effective user scheduling and robust precoding under imperfect Channel State Information (CSI), which are typically optimized separately in a suboptimal manner. A unified, adaptive solution is needed to fully exploit RSMA's potential in dynamic, uncertain wireless environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agent, trained to interact with a robust precoding solver as its environment, can learn a joint user scheduling and stream assignment policy that explicitly accounts for physical-layer transmission constraints, thereby outperforming traditional decoupled optimization approaches in terms of long-term sum-rate under CSI uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) where the state includes channel estimates and error statistics, and actions are user selection and common/private stream assignments. A DRL agent, likely based on a Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) or Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) architecture, will output scheduling decisions. These decisions will be fed into a state-of-the-art robust precoding solver (e.g., based on convex approximations or successive convex programming) which computes the actual precoders and achievable rates, providing a reward to the agent. The agent will be trained end-to-end via this closed-loop interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first unified DRL-based framework for joint scheduling and robust precoding in RSMA, providing a significant performance gain over decoupled baselines. It will also yield a practical, adaptive algorithm for real-time operation in dynamic networks with imperfect CSI, advancing the application of agentic AI in future intelligent communications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating the robust precoding solver as a dynamic, non-differentiable component of the DRL environment, enabling the scheduler to learn from the physical-layer outcomes directly. This creates a truly end-to-end learning loop for RSMA resource allocation, a paradigm shift from the conventional sequential optimization.

proposals.title: Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Model-Agnostic Robust RSMA in Finite Blocklength URLLC
proposals.motivation: URLLC in 5G/6G requires ultra-reliable packet delivery under strict latency, forcing operation in the finite blocklength (FBL) regime where classical Shannon capacity formulas break down. Existing robust RSMA designs for FBL rely on precise statistical models for channel uncertainty and error-rate approximations, which are fragile to model mismatches and real-world non-stationarities. This gap limits the practical deployment of RSMA for mission-critical applications where reliability guarantees must be maintained despite unknown or time-varying impairments.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) agent, trained over a broad distribution of potential channel uncertainty models and FBL conditions, can learn a robust RSMA policy that quickly adapts to novel, unseen scenarios with minimal online pilots, providing model-agnostic reliability guarantees that outperform model-based robust optimization.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-RL framework where the inner loop is a model-free RL agent (e.g., proximal policy optimization) that optimizes RSMA precoding and rate-splitting based on limited pilot observations and ACK/NACK feedback. The outer meta-learning loop, using an algorithm like Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML), will train the agent's initial parameters over a synthesized distribution of uncertainty models (e.g., varying error bounds, temporal correlation, non-Gaussian noise) and blocklengths. This equips the agent with priors for rapid online adaptation. Performance will be evaluated using the meta-testing framework on unseen channel models, with reliability measured via outage probability in the FBL regime.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel meta-RL methodology for robust communication system design that decouples performance guarantees from specific analytical models. 2) Demonstrated superiority in outage probability for RSMA in FBL-URLLC under realistic, non-stationary channel uncertainties compared to state-of-the-art robust convex optimization baselines. 3) A framework that bridges the gap between data-driven intelligent communications and provable reliability requirements.
proposals.novelty: The integration of meta-learning with RL for robust RSMA design is novel, moving beyond single-environment RL or model-based robust optimization to achieve model-agnostic reliability. This approach fundamentally addresses the fragility of assuming known uncertainty sets or accurate FBL error models, a key limitation in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Federated Learning for Robust RSMA Precoding in Finite Blocklength Mega-Constellations
proposals.motivation: Next-generation mega-constellations aim to provide global, low-latency connectivity for mission-critical and short-packet IoT services, operating under stringent finite blocklength (FBL) constraints. Centralized optimization of robust Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) precoders across thousands of dynamic satellites is computationally infeasible, while conventional distributed methods lack the scalability and robustness to imperfect CSI needed for reliable FBL performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical federated learning (HFL) framework can scalably learn robust RSMA precoding policies that adapt to local channel distributions and traffic patterns, thereby meeting the ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) requirements of FBL regimes in mega-constellations under imperfect CSI.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-tier HFL architecture where satellites within an orbital plane or cluster act as local learners, training neural network-based precoding models on local FBL rate expressions under CSI uncertainty. Intra-cluster model aggregation is performed by designated cluster heads (e.g., inter-satellite links), followed by inter-cluster aggregation at ground stations or high-altitude platforms. The training will leverage a robust optimization loss function, incorporating metrics like the meta distribution of the FBL rate, and utilize efficient FL algorithms (e.g., FedAvg, FedProx) adapted for the highly heterogeneous and intermittent connectivity of the satellite network.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first scalable, learning-based framework for robust RSMA in FBL mega-constellations, directly addressing the joint challenge of scalability and robustness. It will provide concrete algorithmic designs and convergence analysis for HFL in dynamic non-terrestrial networks, advancing the practical deployment of RSMA for global URLLC services.
proposals.novelty: The novel fusion of HFL for scalable distributed training with the design of robust RSMA precoders specifically tailored for the FBL regime in mega-constellations is unexplored, moving beyond terrestrial-centric FL and asymptotic information-theoretic analyses.

proposals.title: Energy-Optimal Neural Surrogates for Large-Scale MIMO and Distributed Systems via Resource-Abundant Coordination
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of massive MIMO, ultra-dense networks, and distributed compute clusters creates systems with abundant, but energy-intensive, resources. Pre-2026 optimization often focuses on maximizing spectral efficiency or throughput under power constraints, but this paradigm fails to minimize absolute energy consumption when resources are plentiful. A fundamental shift is needed to achieve sustainable operation at scale, moving from resource-constrained to resource-abundant optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep neural network, trained as a surrogate for a high-dimensional, non-convex joint optimization, can discover and map system states to configurations that minimize total energy consumption per computational or communication task by intelligently activating only necessary subsets of abundant resources.
proposals.method: We will formulate a joint optimization problem minimizing total energy for a target performance (e.g., sum-rate, inference accuracy) in a massive MIMO or distributed sensor network. A Graph Neural Network (GNN) or Transformer-based surrogate model will be trained via deep supervised learning on offline solutions generated by iterative algorithms (e.g., fractional programming, Majorization-Minimization). The model will learn to output optimal binary activation vectors for resource elements (antennas, sensors, servers) and continuous parameters (precoding, power allocation) in real-time. Training will incorporate techniques like Gumbel-Softmax for differentiable subset selection.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, real-time control framework for sustainable large-scale systems, demonstrating significant energy savings over conventional always-on or threshold-based schemes. It will provide a methodological bridge between classical iterative optimization and agentic AI for physical system control, explicitly inverting the scarcity assumption to target absolute energy minimization.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the explicit shift from spectral/energy efficiency to absolute energy minimization per task in resource-abundant systems, enabled by a neural surrogate that learns to perform intelligent, dynamic resource subset activation—a problem largely unexplored in pre-2026 communications and systems literature focused on constrained optimization.

proposals.title: Proactive Interference Management for Drone Swarm Compute Migration: A GATE-HPPO and Interference-Aligned RSMA Co-Design
proposals.motivation: Future drone swarms performing collaborative tasks require dynamic compute offloading, but service migration generates bursty, latency-critical traffic that can destabilize the underlying ad-hoc network. Existing Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) designs manage interference reactively, while existing service migration policies often treat the communication layer as a static, unreliable pipe. This gap leads to dropped migration tasks and reduced swarm efficiency in highly mobile environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tight co-design, where a predictive migration agent (GATE-HPPO) informs the interference alignment strategy of RSMA precoders in real-time, will create a protected signal subspace for migration traffic, thereby significantly improving the reliability and rate of dynamic compute offloading in mobile drone networks.
proposals.method: We will first formulate a joint optimization problem for migration decision-making and communication rate maximization under dynamic topology constraints. The GATE-HPPO framework, with its Graph Attention (GATE) network for spatial-temporal prediction and Hybrid Proximal Policy Optimization (HPPO) for decision-making, will be adapted to output not just migration actions but also interference subspace priorities. These priorities will directly parameterize the design of the RSMA common and private stream precoders, enforcing proactive interference alignment for predicted high-priority migration links. The system will be trained end-to-end in a simulated dynamic drone network environment.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, integrated framework for joint communication and computation control in mobile ad-hoc networks. It will provide concrete performance gains in terms of migration success rate and overall network throughput. The resulting algorithm and analysis will serve as a blueprint for embedding communication-aware intelligence into multi-agent robotic systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, proactive fusion of a predictive AI agent with physical-layer precoder design, moving beyond treating RSMA as a fixed-access scheme or the network as a black-box channel for migration logic.

proposals.title: Decentralized Agentic Learning for Large AI Models in 6G: A Federated Optimization Framework with Communication-Rate Efficiency
proposals.motivation: The development of Large AI Models (LAMs) for future intelligent communication systems (e.g., 6G) relies on massive, diverse datasets, often containing sensitive user information. Centralized collection of this data creates significant privacy risks, regulatory hurdles, and unsustainable communication overhead. A paradigm shift is needed to build performant LAMs while respecting data locality and network constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated, agentic AI framework, co-designed with communication-efficient protocols and robust optimization, can train foundational LAM components for wireless systems without data centralization, achieving performance comparable to centralized training while preserving privacy and reducing network load.
proposals.method: We propose a novel federated learning (FL) architecture where intelligent agents at network edges (base stations, RIS/FAS controllers) collaboratively train a global LAM. The core technical approach integrates: 1) A temporal correlation-aware client selection algorithm to optimize the learning rate and system efficiency. 2) Adaptive model compression and over-the-air computation techniques, informed by rate-distortion theory, to minimize communication rounds and bandwidth. 3) A robust aggregation scheme, inspired by RSMA precoding designs, to mitigate the impact of heterogeneous data and straggling devices. Performance will be evaluated on tasks like channel prediction and resource allocation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational framework for privacy-preserving, scalable LAM development in 6G. It will deliver new optimization algorithms for federated agentic systems and quantify the fundamental trade-offs between learning accuracy, communication cost, and convergence time in a wireless context.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard FL, this framework explicitly co-designs the learning algorithm with physical-layer communication strategies (e.g., over-the-air computation) and robust multi-user optimization principles from RSMA, tailored for the unique temporal and structural patterns of wireless data.

proposals.title: Agentic LAMs for Dynamic Semantic Alignment in Next-Generation Networks: A Meta-Optimization Framework
proposals.motivation: Future intelligent networks (6G, semantic communications) assume stable semantic models, but user intent, protocol meaning, and environmental context evolve rapidly, creating a semantic drift problem. This mismatch degrades the performance of Large AI Models (LAMs) used for network optimization, as they are trained on static data distributions, leading to unreliable control and suboptimal resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agentic LAM architecture, equipped with a meta-optimization loop for online semantic grounding, can dynamically align with and adapt to rapidly shifting network semantics, thereby maintaining optimal communication rates and system robustness where static models fail.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier agentic architecture: a core LAM for network optimization tasks (e.g., robust RSMA precoding, RIS configuration) and a lightweight meta-agent monitoring semantic drift via temporal contrastive learning on protocol streams and user feedback. The meta-agent will use a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) framework to periodically generate lightweight adapter modules, fine-tuning the core LAM's semantic understanding without catastrophic forgetting. This will be integrated with a digital twin of an integrated RIS-FAS system for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for LAMs that are resilient to semantic drift in dynamic networks, a set of metrics for quantifying semantic alignment, and demonstrated performance gains in rate and robustness for use cases like RSMA and RIS-aided systems. It bridges the gap between static AI/ML models and the agentic AI paradigm for communications.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that optimizes physical/logical layers assuming fixed semantics, this inverts that assumption, making the semantic model itself the primary optimization variable. It moves beyond using LAMs as static function approximators to treating them as adaptive, agentic systems within the network control loop.

proposals.title: Heterogeneous Agentic AI for 6G Networks: A Multi-Task Optimization Framework for Control and User Plane Functions
proposals.motivation: The vision of agentic AI for future intelligent networks, as outlined in recent tutorials, currently leans on homogeneous Large AI Model (LAM) architectures. This one-size-fits-all approach is inefficient for the diverse functional split between control-plane tasks (e.g., resource orchestration, robust precoding) and user-plane tasks (e.g., real-time beamforming, rate adaptation). Pre-2026, optimizing systems like RIS-FAS and RSMA-MIMO requires distinct temporal scales and optimization criteria, which a monolithic agent struggles to address simultaneously.
proposals.hypothesis: A heterogeneous multi-agent architecture, where specialized agents are co-designed for distinct network functions, will achieve superior performance in terms of convergence rate, robustness, and overall system utility compared to a homogeneous agentic AI baseline.
proposals.method: We propose a hierarchical framework with two specialized agent classes. For control-plane tasks (e.g., RSMA precoding, RIS configuration), we employ a Transformer-based agent trained via constrained multi-objective optimization (e.g., using Lagrangian methods for robustness). For latency-critical user-plane tasks (e.g., adaptive modulation and coding), we design lightweight, recurrent neural network-based agents trained via deep reinforcement learning for fast temporal adaptation. A meta-controller, formulated as a contextual bandit problem, will dynamically allocate tasks and manage inter-agent information exchange.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, optimized framework for deploying heterogeneous agentic AI in communication systems, providing concrete performance gains in key metrics like spectral efficiency and reliability. It will also establish a principled methodology for task-aware AI specialization in networks, moving beyond generic LAM application.
proposals.novelty: While agentic AI is emerging, its application remains homogeneous. This work is the first to systematically co-design heterogeneous AI agents aligned with the fundamental control/user-plane split in communication systems, creating a tailored optimization and temporal processing hierarchy.

proposals.title: Agentic AI for Robust Wireless Network Control Under Imperfect Perception: A Rate-Distortion-Optimized Multi-Agent Learning Framework
proposals.motivation: Future intelligent networks, as envisioned in 6G and agentic AI frameworks, will rely on autonomous agents to manage resources. However, real-world perception of network state (e.g., channel conditions, user locations) is inherently partial, delayed, and noisy due to measurement limitations and signaling overhead. Existing optimization and learning models often assume perfect global state information, leading to brittle performance in practical deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the perception process as a rate-constrained, noisy observation channel within a multi-agent Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) will yield more robust and communication-efficient control policies, significantly improving long-term network utility compared to agents trained on idealized state assumptions.
proposals.method: We will formulate a multi-agent POMDP where each agent receives local observations distorted by a parametric noise model and delayed by a stochastic process. The core technical approach integrates: 1) A perception module, inspired by rate-distortion theory, that learns a compressed and predictive representation of the hidden network state from noisy inputs, optimizing the trade-off between perception accuracy and signaling cost. 2) A multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) algorithm, building on MADDPG or QMIX, where agents' critics are conditioned on these learned representations. 3) An outer-loop optimization, using techniques from our prior work on LAMS (Learning and Meta-Learning Strategies), to jointly adapt the perception and action policies under varying perception quality constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified framework for designing perception-aware agentic AI in communications. It will provide new algorithms for robust multi-agent learning under imperfect information and derive fundamental performance bounds linking perception quality (rate-distortion) to achievable network control utility, offering practical design guidelines for future intelligent systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of information-theoretic perception modeling (rate-distortion) directly into the MARL loop for network control, moving beyond simple observation noise injection. This creates a co-design of perception, compression, and action, which is largely unexplored in pre-2026 intelligent communications research.

proposals.title: Agentic LAMs for 6G: A Foundation Model for Joint Optimization of Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces and Rate-Splitting Multiple Access
proposals.motivation: The vision for 6G involves ultra-dense, intelligent networks integrating complex technologies like Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS) and Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA). Pre-2026, optimizing these systems jointly in real-time under dynamic channel conditions and user demands is a high-dimensional, non-convex problem that exceeds the scalability of conventional optimization. Large AI Models (LAMs) show emergent reasoning capabilities but are not specialized for the precise, constrained optimization tasks of physical-layer communication.
proposals.hypothesis: A specialized, physics-informed LAM, trained on a synthetically generated corpus of network optimization problems, can learn to act as an agentic orchestrator that outperforms traditional iterative algorithms in jointly optimizing RIS configurations and RSMA precoding for sum-rate maximization under latency constraints.
proposals.method: We propose to develop a transformer-based LAM architecture where the input sequence encodes the network state (channel matrices, user QoS demands, constraints) and the output sequence dictates RIS phase shifts and RSMA precoding matrices. The model will be pre-trained via supervised learning on a massive dataset generated by solving varied instances of the optimization problem using a novel Lagrangian Alternating Minimization Solver (LAMS). It will then be fine-tuned with reinforcement learning, where the reward function is the achieved sum-rate, enabling agentic, real-time adaptation. The training will incorporate physical layer constraints directly into the loss function.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source, foundational LAM specifically for 6G radio resource management, capable of zero-shot generalization to unseen network topologies. It will provide a new paradigm for real-time network optimization, moving from problem-specific solvers to a single, adaptable agentic model, significantly reducing computational latency for decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While LAMs are being explored for network control, this proposal uniquely focuses on creating a foundational model for the core physical-layer joint optimization of RIS and RSMA—a highly complex, coupled problem—by integrating domain-specific solver-generated data (LAMS) and constraint-aware architecture, moving beyond generic LLM applications.

proposals.title: COMM-LAM: A Large-Scale, Multi-Modal Dataset and Benchmark for Training Communication-Specific Large AI Models
proposals.motivation: The emergence of Large AI Models (LAMs) for intelligent communication systems, such as agentic networks and RIS optimization, is currently bottlenecked by a lack of comprehensive, large-scale datasets. Existing datasets are often narrow in scope, focusing on isolated aspects like channel state information (CSI) or traffic traces, which prevents LAMs from learning the complex, temporal interdependencies between network states, user behaviors, and protocol dynamics. Building a foundational dataset is critical to move beyond proof-of-concept LAM applications and achieve robust, generalizable AI-native communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a large-scale, multi-modal dataset capturing synchronized network traffic, multi-antenna channel states, and protocol control signals will enable the training of LAMs that can discover novel, non-intuitive optimization policies for rate maximization and resource allocation, surpassing the performance of model-based and conventional learning-based methods.
proposals.method: We will construct the dataset by instrumenting a large-scale network testbed (e.g., OpenAirInterface) and conducting extensive ray-tracing simulations. The dataset will integrate three synchronized modalities: 1) high-dimensional, temporal CSI matrices from multi-user MIMO/RIS setups, 2) packet-level network traffic with protocol metadata, and 3) system control actions and performance metrics (e.g., SINR, rate). We will employ a graph-based data schema to represent network topology and user associations. For benchmarking, we will define tasks like predictive CSI completion, proactive handover, and joint precoding/traffic optimization, using baseline algorithms from Debbah's prior work on RSMA robust precoding and 3D Gaussian field modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open, large-scale benchmark for communication LAMs, accelerating research in AI-native network design. It will enable the community to train and compare models on standardized tasks, moving the field from isolated simulations to reproducible, data-driven foundation models. The dataset will directly facilitate the development of the agentic AI systems discussed in Debbah's tutorial, providing the necessary experiential data for training.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing datasets, COMM-LAM integrates the physical layer (channel states), network layer (traffic), and control plane (protocol actions) into a unified, temporally aligned resource, specifically designed for end-to-end LAM training rather than single-task learning. Its graph-structured representation and defined benchmark tasks for cross-layer optimization are novel contributions to the field.

proposals.title: Joint Architecture and Communication Optimization for Agentic LAM Inference at the Network Edge
proposals.motivation: The deployment of Large AI Models (LAMs) for agentic tasks in future intelligent networks (e.g., autonomous coordination, real-time sensing) is hindered by the severe computational and latency constraints of edge devices. Current model compression techniques often degrade performance in dynamic environments, and existing edge computing frameworks treat model optimization and network resource allocation as separate problems, leading to suboptimal end-to-end inference rates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a co-design framework, jointly optimizing a lightweight, temporally-aware LAM architecture and the multi-access communication protocol, can achieve a superior latency-accuracy trade-off for real-time agentic inference at the edge compared to sequential optimization approaches.
proposals.method: We propose a bilevel optimization problem. First, we design a lightweight LAM backbone using neural architecture search (NAS) guided by a novel temporal sparsity metric to favor structures efficient for sequential agentic tasks. Second, we integrate this model into a rate-distortion optimization loop, treating the model's activation maps as correlated sources. We will leverage RSMA (Rate-Splitting Multiple Access) as the physical layer, optimizing robust precoders to minimize the wireless transmission distortion of these activations under channel uncertainty, thereby maximizing the effective inference rate.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a systematic methodology for co-designing LAM architecture and communication for edge agentic AI, 2) a concrete optimization framework merging NAS with robust RSMA precoding, and 3) quantitative benchmarks for latency-accuracy trade-offs in simulated dynamic edge scenarios, providing a foundation for future 6G intelligent systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight coupling of temporal LAM sparsity structure design with communication-aware robust precoding optimization, moving beyond independent model compression or link adaptation, specifically tailored for the sequential decision-making requirements of agentic AI.

proposals.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration for Dynamic Network Slicing: A Hierarchical MARL Framework with Contract Theory
proposals.motivation: The evolution towards 6G and Open RAN demands intelligent, real-time management of heterogeneous resources (e.g., RIS, FAS, MIMO) across multiple network slices. Centralized optimization struggles with scalability and latency, while independent agents risk conflicting actions and sub-optimal global performance. A principled framework for collaborative, decentralized AI-driven control is therefore critical for future intelligent communication systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) architecture, augmented with contract-theoretic incentive mechanisms, can enable stable, efficient, and scalable collaboration among autonomous agents for network slicing, leading to superior long-term sum-rate and reliability compared to both centralized and non-cooperative decentralized baselines.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier architecture: a meta-controller agent at the network orchestrator level and multiple subordinate agents managing individual slices or geographical regions. The framework will integrate Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for agent observation encoding to handle network topology. Collaboration will be enforced and incentivized using a multi-agent extension of Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) with a shared critic, combined with contract theory to design reward-shaping mechanisms that align local agent actions (e.g., precoding, RIS configuration) with global network utility (e.g., weighted sum-rate, QoS fairness).
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel, scalable MARL framework for collaborative network management that bridges agentic AI and communication systems optimization. 2) A formal integration of economic incentive design (contract theory) into MARL training to ensure stable coalition formation among self-interested network agents. 3) Quantitative benchmarks demonstrating performance gains in dynamic network slicing scenarios over state-of-the-art baselines.
proposals.novelty: While MARL has been explored for networks, the explicit combination of hierarchical GNN-based MARL with microeconomic contract design to engineer cooperative behavior among intelligent agents in a physical-layer-aware resource management problem is a novel synthesis of Prof. Debbah's expertise in optimization, temporal systems, and large AI models.

proposals.title: Knowledge-Augmented Large AI Models for Dynamic Network Optimization: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework
proposals.motivation: Large AI Models (LAMs) show promise for intelligent network control but lack precise, verifiable knowledge of physical layer constraints and protocol states, leading to unreliable or suboptimal decisions. Integrating structured, domain-specific knowledge bases (KBs) with LAMs' reasoning capabilities is critical for robust, real-time optimization in complex systems like RIS-aided or RSMA-based networks, where decisions must respect strict temporal and spectral constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: A neuro-symbolic architecture that grounds a LAM's reasoning in a structured, updatable network knowledge base will yield more reliable, explainable, and temporally consistent control policies than a purely data-driven LAM, significantly improving key performance indicators like weighted sum-rate and outage probability.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier architecture: a symbolic KB encoding network topology, channel models, protocol specifications, and optimization constraints (e.g., from 3D Gaussian field models or RSMA rate regions). A LAM (e.g., a fine-tuned transformer) acts as a reasoning engine, querying the KB via a structured interface. For a given network state, the LAM generates candidate actions, which are validated and refined by a constraint solver against the KB. We will develop a training loop using reinforcement learning, where rewards are based on communication metrics, and penalties are imposed for KB constraint violations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for knowledge-augmented AI in communications, a benchmark for reliable LAM-based network control, and concrete performance gains in case studies on RIS configuration and robust RSMA precoding. It will provide a blueprint for moving from reactive to proactive, semantically-grounded network intelligence.
proposals.novelty: While LAMs and KBs exist separately, their tight integration for real-time, constraint-aware communication system optimization is unexplored. The novelty lies in the specific neuro-symbolic co-design that uses the KB not just for retrieval but for formal action validation, ensuring physical-layer feasibility.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Multi-Agent Orchestration for Continent-Scale Resource Optimization in 6G Networks
proposals.motivation: The vision for 6G and future intelligent communication systems involves the pervasive deployment of millions of AI agents for tasks like network slicing, integrated sensing, and autonomous system control. Pre-2026, orchestrating such a vast, heterogeneous, and temporally dynamic multi-agent system (MAS) at a continental scale presents a fundamental challenge in scalability, stability, and global optimization, which existing cloud-centric or fully decentralized approaches cannot adequately solve.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical, hybrid-centralized/decentralized orchestration architecture, leveraging Large AI Models (LAMs) for high-level intent translation and multi-agent reinforcement learning for local coordination, can achieve stable, scalable, and near-optimal global resource allocation across a continent-scale network of millions of agents.
proposals.method: We propose a three-tier architecture: 1) A central 'orchestrator LAM' trained on network-wide data translates high-level service intents into regional performance targets. 2) Regional meta-controllers, formulated as a Mean-Field Game (MFG), optimize aggregate agent behaviors using robust precoding and RSMA-inspired resource splitting to manage interference and uncertainty. 3) Local clusters of agents employ a novel distributed algorithm, 'Temporally-Aware Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization' (TA-MAPPO), which incorporates learned temporal abstractions from the upper tiers to coordinate actions over extended time horizons. Stability will be analyzed using Lyapunov drift-plus-penalty methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational framework and stability proofs for continent-scale MAS orchestration. It will deliver novel algorithms for hierarchical, temporally-extended multi-agent reinforcement learning and demonstrate their application in a holistic 6G network optimization simulator, setting a benchmark for scalability in intelligent communications.
proposals.novelty: The integration of an intent-based LAM orchestrator with a hierarchical MFG and MARL structure specifically designed for the extreme scale and heterogeneity of 6G systems is novel, moving beyond single-system optimization to continent-wide, multi-service holistic coordination.

proposals.title: Joint Communication and Computation Optimization for Distributed Large AI Model Training over 6G Semantic-Aware Networks
proposals.motivation: Training Large AI Models (LAMs) is bottlenecked by massive computational and data transfer requirements. Emerging 6G networks, with integrated sensing, communication, and computing, offer a dynamic fabric of heterogeneous resources but lack a unified framework to co-optimize distributed training workflows with the network's time-varying semantic and physical state. This gap prevents realizing the full potential of agentic AI and intelligent communication systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the 6G network as a dynamic, semantic-aware constraint system and co-optimizing task distribution, gradient synchronization, and radio resource allocation will significantly improve training convergence time and energy efficiency compared to static or computation-only scheduling.
proposals.method: We propose a two-timescale optimization problem. At a slow timescale, we formulate a stochastic optimization problem to partition LAM layers and training batches across network nodes (RIS-assisted, MIMO-equipped, edge servers) based on long-term compute profiles. At a fast timescale, we design a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework, where intelligent agents at network controllers use real-time channel state information (from 3D radiation field models) and semantic data relevance to dynamically allocate radio resources (using RSMA-based robust precoding) for gradient aggregation, minimizing communication latency. The Lyapunov optimization technique will ensure stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel joint communication-computation optimization framework for distributed LAM training over 6G, 2) a practical MARL-based algorithm for real-time resource allocation that accounts for semantic value and network dynamics, and 3) quantitative benchmarks showing reduced time-to-accuracy and improved energy efficiency in heterogeneous network simulations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the dual feedback loop: using the network's intelligent communication fabric to optimize AI training distribution, while simultaneously using the semantic and temporal demands of the AI training task to optimize the network's physical layer (e.g., RIS configuration, RSMA precoding), moving beyond treating the network as a passive pipe.

proposals.title: Robust Ground Infrastructure Planning for LEO Constellations: A Two-Stage Stochastic Optimization Framework for User Placement under Orbital Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of low-Earth orbit (LEO) mega-constellations promises global broadband coverage but introduces significant operational complexity due to stochastic orbital perturbations, potential satellite failures, and the need for seamless service migration. Current network planning often treats ground station placement as static, decoupled from the probabilistic evolution of the constellation state, leading to suboptimal resilience and service disruption. A unified framework is needed to co-design infrastructure strategy with operational policies, ensuring robust performance against inherent orbital uncertainties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling ground user/gateway placement as a first-stage decision variable, coupled with a second-stage service migration policy that reacts to stochastic orbital dynamics, will yield a significantly more robust and cost-effective network design than traditional deterministic or decoupled approaches.
proposals.method: We will formulate a two-stage stochastic optimization problem. The first stage decides the geographical placement of ground stations. The second stage, for each sampled orbital scenario (including perturbations and failures), optimizes the service migration policy (e.g., user-satellite association, handover strategy) to maintain connectivity and data rate. We will leverage the Sample Average Approximation (SAA) method with scenario generation from high-fidelity orbital mechanics models (e.g., SGP4). The resulting large-scale mixed-integer program will be solved using decomposition techniques like Benders decomposition or modern large-scale convex optimization solvers, informed by Dr. Debbah's expertise in robust precoding and RSMA designs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel mathematical framework for joint infrastructure and operational resilience planning in non-geostationary satellite networks, 2) quantitative benchmarks demonstrating the value of stochastic co-optimization over deterministic planning, and 3) scalable algorithmic solutions for this high-dimensional stochastic integer program.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the traditional problem by making user placement a decision variable optimized jointly with a reactive service migration policy under explicit stochastic orbital dynamics, a synergy absent in prior isolated studies of placement or handover optimization.

proposals.title: Proactive Scheduling for RSMA Networks via Non-Markovian Mobility Modeling and Low-Complexity Optimization
proposals.motivation: Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) is a powerful framework for managing interference in multiuser MIMO systems, but its practical deployment is hindered by the high computational cost of dynamic scheduling and precoding optimization. Concurrently, user mobility in real networks exhibits long-term temporal dependencies and habitual patterns that are poorly captured by standard Markovian models, leading to suboptimal and reactive resource allocation. Bridging this gap is crucial for developing efficient, real-world intelligent communication systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating predictive, non-Markovian models of user mobility into the RSMA scheduling problem will enable the design of low-complexity, proactive algorithms that maintain high spectral efficiency and QoS by anticipating interference patterns, thereby significantly reducing the need for instantaneous full-scale optimization.
proposals.method: We will first develop a mobility prediction model using Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks or Transformers to capture long-term dependencies in user trajectories from real-world datasets. This model will forecast future user positions and cluster stability. Second, we will design a two-timescale scheduling framework: a slow-timescale proactive scheduler that uses mobility predictions to pre-compute user grouping and coarse power splits for common/private streams, and a fast-timescale lightweight adaptive module for fine-tuning based on instantaneous CSI. Optimization will leverage tools like fractional programming and deep reinforcement learning, trained on the predicted mobility patterns to minimize long-term expected interference.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, practical RSMA scheduling framework that explicitly accounts for real-world mobility with memory. It will provide a significant reduction in online computational complexity compared to state-of-the-art instantaneous optimization methods while demonstrating robustness and gains in spectral efficiency and latency via simulation in standardized 3GPP scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of non-Markovian, memory-dependent mobility modeling with RSMA resource allocation is unexplored. This moves beyond conventional reactive optimization or simple Markov models, creating a uniquely predictive and low-complexity scheduler that bridges advanced AI-based trajectory forecasting with communication-theoretic signal design.

proposals.title: Eco-Inspired Adaptive Task Offloading for Heterogeneous LEO Satellite Constellations: A Resource Partitioning Framework
proposals.motivation: The computational layer of emerging LEO mega-constellations is often abstracted as a homogeneous cloud, ignoring the inherent heterogeneity in satellite hardware, orbital position, and available power. This leads to inefficient resource utilization, increased intra-constellation communication overhead, and suboptimal task completion rates. A novel scheduling paradigm is needed to transform this hardware diversity from a management burden into a source of systemic resilience and efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling a satellite constellation as a computational ecosystem and applying the ecological principle of resource partitioning, we can design a distributed task offloading framework that significantly improves total system processing throughput and energy efficiency compared to homogeneous resource pooling strategies.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a multi-agent, temporal graph optimization where nodes (satellites) have dynamically evolving compute, storage, and power profiles. Inspired by niche differentiation, we will develop a hierarchical, intention-aware scheduling algorithm. Lightweight 'forager' agents (e.g., CubeSats) will use a learning-aided matching scheme (LAMS) for initial sensing/relay tasks. A distributed auction mechanism, informed by predicted resource states, will route intermediate tasks to 'generalist' nodes, while complex analytics are offloaded to high-capability 'specialist' hubs, optimizing the end-to-end processing chain.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel ecosystemic model for heterogeneous space-based computing, 2) a suite of distributed, low-overhead algorithms for intention-aware task partitioning and routing, and 3) a rigorous analysis of the trade-offs between specialization, load balancing, and communication cost in temporal networks, validated through satellite network simulations.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct translation of ecological resource partitioning principles into a rigorous optimization and communication framework for satellite networks, moving beyond homogeneous or simply hierarchical models to an adaptive, role-based specialization that exploits hardware diversity as a first-order design feature.

proposals.title: Adaptive Temporal Attention for Resource-Aware Scheduling in Heterogeneous LEO Satellite Constellations
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of heterogeneous low-Earth orbit (LEO) constellations, mixing advanced and legacy satellites, creates a complex resource-constrained scheduling problem. Current methods either ignore long-term temporal dependencies (e.g., orbital patterns, traffic periodicity) crucial for optimal coordination or apply uniform, computationally expensive models that violate the power and compute budgets of less capable nodes. This gap limits the collective efficiency and data rate of the entire constellation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a scheduling framework with a temporal encoder featuring adaptive attention mechanisms—which dynamically scales its computational complexity and historical look-back horizon based on the instantaneous resource profile of the scheduling node—can achieve superior long-term sum-rate performance compared to static schedulers, while guaranteeing per-satellite resource constraints.
proposals.method: We will design a hierarchical scheduler. A central orchestrator runs a full-capacity Transformer temporal encoder to learn long-term patterns. For each heterogeneous satellite, we deploy a lightweight, adaptive attention module. This module uses a gating network, informed by the satellite's real-time compute, storage, and power profile, to dynamically prune attention heads, reduce sequence length, or switch to a more efficient architecture (e.g., Linear Transformer). The scheduling policy will be optimized end-to-end using constrained reinforcement learning (CRL), integrating the adaptive modules with the central model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, resource-aware temporal modeling framework for satellite networks, enabling high-fidelity coordination in heterogeneous systems. It will provide concrete algorithms for adaptive attention and a CRL-based training methodology, advancing the field of intelligent communication systems under strict operational constraints.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the resource-conditional adaptive attention mechanism, which allows a single temporal model to operate in multiple fidelity regimes dynamically. This moves beyond static model compression or knowledge distillation by making adaptation a real-time, context-aware function of the node's instantaneous state.

proposals.title: A Co-Modeling Framework for LEO Satellite Networks: Integrating Physical Optical Links and Protocol Dynamics via Embedded Discrete-Event Simulation
proposals.motivation: Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks promise global connectivity but face a significant model-reality gap. Current optimization models for routing and resource allocation often treat inter-satellite optical links as static pipes with fixed capacity and latency, ignoring the dynamic, contention-driven performance dictated by network protocols (e.g., TCP, scheduling, handshakes). This leads to suboptimal path planning and unreliable service prediction for edge applications. Closing this gap is critical for robust, network-aware operations in next-generation non-terrestrial networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding a lightweight, protocol-aware discrete-event simulator within each node of a network graph model will generate accurate, scenario-specific service profiles (e.g., realized rate, latency distribution) for each link, enabling superior prediction of end-to-end performance and more robust network optimization.
proposals.method: We propose a hierarchical co-modeling framework. At the physical layer, each optical link is characterized by its 3D Gaussian radiation field parameters (extending RIS/FAS modeling). At the network layer, each satellite node hosts a minimal discrete-event simulation kernel modeling protocol state machines, queue scheduling, and traffic contention. For a given routing and traffic load scenario, these embedded simulators interact to generate dynamic service profiles for each graph edge. We will then formulate a robust optimization problem, potentially using Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) inspired precoding for traffic splitting, to optimize path planning and resource allocation based on these profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel simulation-in-the-loop network modeling paradigm that bridges physical and network layers, 2) a set of derived, dynamic service profiles for LEO optical links under realistic protocol constraints, and 3) a demonstration of improved robustness in network-wide optimization problems like latency-aware routing and load balancing, validated against conventional static-graph approaches.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, lightweight integration of discrete-event protocol simulation directly into the graph edges of a network optimization model, moving beyond average parameter abstraction to scenario-specific, protocol-governed performance prediction. This merges Debbah's expertise in physical-layer optimization with agentic simulation concepts for a holistic systems view.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Graph Model for LEO Satellite Networks: Adapting Neurovascular Coupling for Spatio-Temporal Inter-Satellite Link Optimization
proposals.motivation: Future low-Earth orbit (LEO) mega-constellations require intelligent, adaptive networking to manage highly dynamic inter-satellite links (ISLs), where optical link capacity and latency fluctuate due to traffic, pointing errors, and orbital mechanics. Existing graph-based network models often treat these links as static or use simplistic Markov models, failing to capture the intricate, physics-coupled temporal dynamics essential for robust routing and resource allocation in integrated space-air-ground networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the mathematical principles of neurovascular coupling (NVC), specifically the Balloon-Windkessel model, can be formally adapted to accurately model the temporal evolution of ISL capacity and latency as a function of local data demand (neural activity analog) and physical link state (vascular constraints analog), leading to a more predictive dynamic graph model for satellite networks.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel dynamic graph framework where each ISL is governed by a system of coupled differential equations inspired by the Balloon-Windkessel model. The 'flow' (link capacity) and 'volume' (latency/queueing) will be state variables updated by an input 'neural signal' (weighted traffic demand and priority) and constrained by 'vascular' parameters (derived from real-time pointing accuracy, range, and Doppler shift). We will formulate a constrained optimization problem to minimize end-to-end latency under time-varying demand, solving it using a distributed, intelligent agent-based approach where each satellite node runs a lightweight version of the model to enable proactive link feature prediction and network reconfiguration.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A novel, bio-inspired dynamic graph model for satellite networks with temporally evolving edge features, 2) A distributed optimization framework for predictive routing and resource allocation in LEO constellations, enhancing overall network throughput and robustness, and 3) A new interdisciplinary bridge between computational neuroscience models and communication systems theory.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, formal analogy between neurovascular coupling and ISL dynamics, translating a well-established physiological model to a communications context—a cross-disciplinary leap not previously explored. This moves beyond typical queuing or Markov models by incorporating a coupled, physics-informed dynamic system at the link level.

proposals.title: Jointly Robust Cross-Layer Optimization for Multi-Hop LEO-NOMA Constellations with Imperfect SIC and Dynamic Optical ISLs
proposals.motivation: The integration of Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) with dense Low-Earth Orbit (LEO) constellations promises massive connectivity and high spectral efficiency for 6G non-terrestrial networks. However, existing designs treat the physical-layer NOMA imperfections, like imperfect Successive Interference Cancellation (SIC), and the network-layer volatility of optical Inter-Satellite Links (ISLs) as separate problems. This isolation leads to brittle performance where residual interference from imperfect SIC is amplified by unpredictable multi-hop routing delays and capacity fluctuations, degrading end-to-end reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: By jointly modeling the time-varying optical ISL graph and the residual interference from imperfect SIC within a unified stochastic optimization framework, we can derive a cross-layer strategy that makes user grouping, power allocation, and routing decisions inherently robust to both types of imperfections, thereby guaranteeing stricter end-to-end quality of service (QoS) bounds.
proposals.method: We will model the LEO constellation as a temporal graph with stochastic edge features (capacity, latency) for optical ISLs, derived from orbital mechanics and atmospheric disturbance models. This graph will constrain a multi-hop rate maximization problem where user rates are functions of imperfect SIC error propagation, modeled via bounded error variance. We propose a two-tier algorithmic solution: 1) A Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based agent to learn and predict high-quality multi-hop paths, and 2) A successive convex approximation (SCA) and fractional programming-based iterative solver for joint intra-cluster power allocation and user pairing, conditioned on the predicted path states. The integration will use a primal-dual learning approach to balance the network flow and physical-layer interference.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first integrated analytical framework and optimization algorithm for robust multi-hop NOMA in LEO networks. The primary contributions are a novel stochastic cross-layer problem formulation and a practical, iterative solution that jointly adapts to both link dynamics and receiver imperfections, providing rigorous end-to-end latency and rate guarantees validated through system-level simulation.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synergy lies in explicitly using the dynamic, graph-based state of the optical ISL network to inform and constrain the physical-layer NOMA design against imperfect SIC, creating a feedback loop where routing decisions are aware of interference limits and vice-versa, a perspective absent in prior isolated optimizations.

proposals.title: Dynamic Graph-Based Optimization for LEO Satellite Networks with Stochastic Inter-Satellite Optical Links
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large-scale low-Earth orbit (LEO) satellite constellations for global coverage necessitates robust and adaptive routing. Pre-2026, graph models for satellite networks often treat inter-satellite links (ISLs), especially optical, as static or binary, ignoring their highly dynamic capacity and latency due to pointing errors, atmospheric effects, and orbital mechanics. This limits the ability to optimize for stringent end-to-end rate and latency requirements in integrated terrestrial-satellite systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling ISL capacity and latency as dynamic, stochastic edge features within a spatio-temporal graph will enable the design of a robust optimization framework that significantly improves end-to-end communication rate and reliability compared to static-graph routing protocols.
proposals.method: We will extend a dynamic graph neural network (GNN) model where nodes represent satellites and ground stations, and edges encode time-varying ISL features (capacity, latency, availability probability). The dynamics will be learned from orbital mechanics and a statistical model for optical link degradation. We will formulate a constrained optimization problem for joint path selection and flow control, aiming to maximize a utility function of long-term average data rates under latency constraints. This will be solved using a primal-dual learning-aided method (LAMS) approach, integrating the GNN for predictive state estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, tractable optimization framework for dynamic satellite networks with realistic ISL constraints. It will provide concrete algorithms for adaptive routing that are robust to link stochasticity, directly improving quality of service metrics. The resulting models and methods will be a step toward future intelligent and agentic space-air-ground integrated networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the joint stochastic modeling of optical ISL dynamics (capacity and latency) as integral graph edge features and the subsequent integration of this predictive graph model into a learning-aided network utility maximization problem, moving beyond deterministic or connectivity-only graph abstractions common in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Meta-Testing for Robust Satellite Network Management: A Meta-Reinforcement Learning Framework with Real-World Constellation Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of large low-Earth orbit (LEO) constellations introduces extreme dynamics in network topology and user demand, challenging traditional optimization methods. Current AI-driven approaches, like single-agent reinforcement learning (RL), often fail to generalize to unseen orbital patterns or traffic surges, leading to suboptimal resource allocation and service degradation. A framework for robust, few-shot adaptation is critical for the reliable operation of next-generation integrated space-air-ground networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that meta-training a policy on a diverse, historical dataset of real satellite ephemeris and demand traces will allow a core RL agent to learn a prior over the underlying dynamics of satellite networks. This prior will enable rapid, few-shot adaptation to new deployment patterns or demand shifts, outperforming static or conventionally trained RL policies in dynamic real-world conditions.
proposals.method: We propose a Meta-Testing framework built on a meta-reinforcement learning (Meta-RL) loop, specifically Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML). The inner-loop agent will be a Graph Attention Transformer-Enhanced Proximal Policy Optimization (GATE-HPPO) agent, designed to handle the graph-structured satellite network. We will construct a meta-training dataset from real-world sources (e.g., CelesTrak for orbital traces, ITU for regional demand models). Each meta-task corresponds to a specific temporal window of constellation state and demand. The outer loop will optimize the agent's initial parameters for fast adaptation, evaluated on held-out historical scenarios and simulated novel disruptions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practically-grounded meta-learning framework for dynamic satellite network optimization. It will provide a benchmark methodology for testing robustness using real-world historical data. The resulting adaptive agent will demonstrate significant improvements in key performance indicators (e.g., sum-rate, latency) under non-stationary conditions compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of real, historical orbital and demand data into the meta-training objective, moving beyond synthetic or stationary simulations. This 'meta-testing' paradigm shifts validation from a final step to a core component of the training process, explicitly optimizing for robustness to the non-stationarities inherent in real satellite systems.

proposals.title: A Market-Based Hierarchical Multi-Timescale Framework for Resource Orchestration in Satellite-Terrestrial-Cloud Networks
proposals.motivation: The integration of low-earth orbit (LEO) satellite constellations, terrestrial edge networks, and cloud data centers creates a complex, multi-layer computing fabric with vastly different latencies, dynamics, and administrative domains. Pre-2026, monolithic optimization for such systems is intractable and ignores operational independence, while fully decentralized approaches risk inefficiency and instability. A scalable coordination mechanism is urgently needed to realize the vision of global, seamless 6G/B5G network coverage and compute offloading.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical decomposition with multi-timescale optimization, where fast local control is coordinated by a slower-timescale market-based broker, will achieve near-optimal global resource efficiency while preserving tractability, domain autonomy, and realistic signaling constraints.
proposals.method: We propose a three-tier architecture: 1) Fast-timescale layer-specific optimizers (e.g., robust precoding for satellite-terrestrial links, intelligent scheduling for edge servers, load balancing in data centers). 2) A mid-timescale virtual resource market where a central broker, using a primal-dual or Lyapunov-based online algorithm, negotiates inter-layer resource contracts and SLAs based on predicted demand/supply. 3) A slow-timescale learning component (e.g., a Large AI Model for demand forecasting) to refine market rules and pricing. The coordination will be formulated as a multi-agent stochastic optimization problem with delayed and hierarchical information structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, tractable optimization framework for integrated space-air-ground networks, a concrete market mechanism design for inter-domain resource trading, and a set of convergence and performance bounds for the hierarchical system. It directly advances the theory of large-scale network orchestration.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of multi-timescale stochastic optimization with a market-based coordination mechanism specifically tailored for the heterogeneous dynamics and administrative boundaries of satellite-terrestrial-cloud fabrics is a distinct departure from existing monolithic or purely peer-to-peer approaches.

proposals.title: Flux Balance Optimization for Joint Data and Compute Flow in Multi-Layer Satellite-Terrestrial Networks
proposals.motivation: The integration of low-earth orbit (LEO) satellite constellations with terrestrial edge and cloud resources creates a complex, multi-tier network continuum. Pre-2026, optimizing this heterogeneous system for metrics like end-to-end latency and throughput is challenging due to distinct physical constraints and dynamic resource coupling across layers. Existing optimization frameworks often treat these layers separately or use heuristics, lacking a unified, mathematically rigorous model for joint data routing and computational offloading.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the satellite-terrestrial-edge-cloud continuum as a multi-layer resource transformation network analogous to a metabolic system, its optimal joint flow (data and tasks) can be determined via a constrained optimization framework adapted from Flux Balance Analysis (FBA), leading to superior system-wide performance compared to decoupled or heuristic approaches.
proposals.method: We will define a 'network stoichiometry' matrix S, where rows represent network resources (e.g., satellite link capacity, edge server compute) and columns represent 'reactions' (e.g., data transmission, task processing, inter-layer handoff). The flux vector v, representing flow rates, is optimized via linear programming: max c^T v subject to S v = 0 (flow conservation) and lb ≤ v ≤ ub (layer-specific constraints). The objective c can target throughput or QoS. We will extend this core FBA with robust optimization techniques to handle uncertainty in channel states and task arrivals, aligning with the researcher's recent work on robust precoding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unified optimization framework for integrated space-air-ground networks, delivering a principled method for joint flow control. It will contribute a new 'network stoichiometry' modeling paradigm and demonstrate quantifiable gains in system utility and robustness over baseline methods in simulated LEO-terrestrial scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct conceptual and mathematical translation of Flux Balance Analysis from systems biology to communication and computing network optimization, creating a rigorous analogy between metabolite fluxes and joint data-compute flows across radically different physical layers.

proposals.title: Physics-Constrained Neural Orchestration for Joint Optimization in Multi-Layer Satellite-Terrestrial Edge Networks
proposals.motivation: The integration of low-earth orbit (LEO) satellite constellations with terrestrial networks promises global, low-latency edge computing. However, the extreme dynamism of satellite topologies, governed by orbital mechanics, makes joint optimization of communication and computation resources highly complex. Existing data-driven or heuristic schedulers often violate physical feasibility, leading to suboptimal performance and unreliable service guarantees.
proposals.hypothesis: Embedding the hard constraints of orbital mechanics directly into a neural network's architecture will produce a physics-aware orchestration agent capable of generating resource allocation and routing decisions that are inherently feasible, thereby significantly outperforming conventional optimization and pure deep reinforcement learning (DRL) methods in latency and reliability.
proposals.method: We will design a hybrid neural architecture where a physics-embedding layer encodes satellite ephemeris using parameterized orbital equations (e.g., SGP4 model), ensuring all predicted link states and visibility windows are physically realizable. This layer's outputs will condition a deep graph neural network (GNN) modeling the multi-layer network topology. The combined model will be trained using a Lagrangian dual method, integrating the physics constraints as penalties, to solve a joint optimization problem for computation offloading, data routing, and power/bandwidth allocation. The optimization core will leverage techniques from rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) for robust multi-user MIMO links in the system.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) A novel physics-informed machine learning (PIML) framework for dynamic network orchestration, 2) A set of algorithms for joint communication-computation optimization in integrated satellite-terrestrial systems with guaranteed constraint satisfaction, and 3) Quantitative benchmarks demonstrating superior latency and resource efficiency over state-of-the-art heuristic and DRL baselines.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the architectural fusion of explicit, hard-coded orbital mechanics constraints with a learnable network optimization model, moving beyond soft penalty-based PIML. This ensures all orchestration decisions are physically grounded from first principles, a significant departure from purely data-driven or simplified mobility-model approaches in existing literature.

proposals.title: Joint Orchestration of Communication and Computation in Multi-Layer Satellite-Terrestrial Networks: A Rate-Optimal Framework
proposals.motivation: The integration of low-earth orbit (LEO) satellite constellations with terrestrial networks is a cornerstone of pre-2026 6G visions, promising global coverage and low latency. However, existing optimization models treat satellite and terrestrial resources in isolation, failing to exploit the synergistic potential of joint communication (via RIS/FAS) and distributed computation (across edge servers and data centers) for latency-critical and data-intensive applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified optimization framework, jointly managing radio resources (via RSMA precoding and RIS/FAS configuration) and computational task offloading across the satellite-terrestrial continuum, can achieve significant gains in end-to-end system rate and latency over decoupled approaches.
proposals.method: We will extend the 3D Gaussian radiation field model for RIS/FAS to a multi-layer topology. The joint problem of user association, precoding (RSMA for robustness), RIS phase-shift optimization, and computational offloading routing will be formulated as a stochastic optimization problem accounting for temporal dynamics (satellite mobility, task arrivals). We will decompose it using Lyapunov optimization (drift-plus-penalty) and Large AI Model (LAM)-inspired multi-agent reinforcement learning for intelligent, distributed decision-making among network layers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-of-its-kind analytical model and algorithmic suite for the co-design of communication and computation in integrated space-air-ground networks. It will deliver concrete performance bounds and a scalable, intelligent orchestration protocol, directly contributing to the standardization of future non-terrestrial networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight coupling of the physical-layer RIS/FAS radiation model with network-layer computational offloading and the application of agentic AI principles for distributed control across the highly dynamic, heterogeneous satellite-terrestrial stack.

proposals.title: Orbital Graph Transformers: Dynamic Pairwise Positional Encoding for Long-Range Dependency Modeling in LEO Constellations
proposals.motivation: The rapid expansion of large low-Earth orbit (LEO) constellations for global communications and sensing introduces unprecedented complexity in network management.
