Pre-2026, optimizing tasks like dynamic routing, collision avoidance, and coordinated beamforming requires capturing intricate, long-range spatio-temporal dependencies between satellites, a challenge for traditional graph neural networks or static graph models. This problem is critical for ensuring the robustness, efficiency, and safety of these large-scale orbital systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a graph transformer architecture, equipped with dynamic pairwise positional encodings (PPEs) inspired by protein structure prediction, will significantly outperform existing methods in modeling long-range dependencies for satellite network optimization tasks. The explicit encoding of evolving 3D geometric relationships will provide a crucial inductive bias for learning effective coordination policies.
proposals.method: We will model the satellite constellation as a dynamic graph where nodes represent satellites with features like state vectors. The core innovation is the design of pairwise positional encodings for the graph transformer's attention mechanism. These PPEs will be computed from the relative 3D position vectors, distances, and visibility intervals (line-of-sight) between satellite pairs in an Earth-Centered Inertial frame, updated per time step. We will adapt the transformer architecture from models like AlphaFold2, injecting these geometric PPEs into the attention keys and queries. The model will be trained end-to-end using reinforcement learning or supervised learning on tasks such as predictive resource allocation or conflict-free scheduling.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, physics-informed graph neural architecture specifically designed for large-scale orbital systems. It will provide a new benchmark for accuracy in long-range dependency modeling for satellite network optimization. The proposed framework will advance the field of intelligent communications and spatial networking by introducing a principled method from computational biology to a core telecommunications challenge.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, principled translation of pairwise positional encoding techniques from protein folding (e.g., AlphaFold2) to the dynamic 3D geometry of satellite constellations, a domain not previously connected. This goes beyond simple graph attention by explicitly and dynamically encoding the physical orbital mechanics into the transformer's relational reasoning.

proposals.title: Dynamic Graph Transformers for Adaptive Beamforming and Resource Allocation in Multi-Satellite Networks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of non-terrestrial networks (NTNs) and mega-constellations introduces extreme dynamism, with rapid changes in user populations, link states, and interference patterns. Existing optimization frameworks, often based on static models or iterative algorithms, struggle with this real-time topological volatility, leading to suboptimal spectral efficiency and latency. An adaptive, learning-based approach that can directly ingest and reason over the dynamic graph structure of the network is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A graph transformer architecture with dynamic node/edge addition/removal mechanisms will outperform static graph neural networks (GNNs) and conventional optimization solvers in joint beamforming and resource allocation tasks for multi-satellite systems, by maintaining globally informed representations that are instantly adaptable to network changes.
proposals.method: We will develop a Dynamic Graph Transformer (DGT) where each satellite, user, and antenna element is a node, and communication/interference links are edges. The core innovation is a dynamic attention module that recalculates attention scores only for affected subgraphs upon topology changes, avoiding full recomputation. We will integrate this with a learnable masking and pooling mechanism to handle variable-sized inputs. The DGT will be trained end-to-end using a Lagrangian multiplier method (LAMS) to optimize a weighted sum-rate objective under power constraints, leveraging simulated datasets of dynamic LEO satellite scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel neural architecture for real-time control of dynamic wireless networks, providing a significant performance uplift in sum-rate and robustness for NTNs. It will also contribute a methodological framework for applying dynamic graph learning to communication systems optimization, bridging the gap between geometric deep learning and information theory.
proposals.novelty: While graph neural networks have been applied to wireless networks, the integration of a transformer with truly dynamic graph operations (node/edge lifecycles) for satellite systems is unexplored. The proposed method's ability to maintain long-range dependencies through attention while adapting topology in real-time distinguishes it from prior static GNNs or recurrent approaches.

proposals.title: Graph Transformer Networks for Long-Range Spatial Encoding in LEO Satellite Constellations: Optimization of Beamforming and Handover
proposals.motivation: The performance of next-generation non-terrestrial networks (NTNs) is critically limited by the need to manage complex, long-range spatial dependencies between satellites in low Earth orbit (LEO) constellations. Traditional graph neural networks (GNNs) struggle to capture these dependencies due to their limited receptive fields, leading to suboptimal resource allocation, beamforming, and handover decisions that degrade system rate and reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a graph transformer network, with its global self-attention mechanism, can directly model the long-range spatial-temporal correlations between distant satellites, enabling superior optimization of network-wide parameters compared to localized GNNs or conventional optimization methods.
proposals.method: We will formulate the LEO constellation as a dynamic spatial graph, where nodes represent satellites with features like position and traffic load, and edges represent potential interference or handover links. A Graph Transformer architecture, incorporating positional encodings based on orbital mechanics, will be applied to this graph. The model will be trained end-to-end to optimize a multi-objective loss function combining sum-rate maximization and handover minimization, using techniques like deep unfolding to integrate with traditional optimization frameworks such as weighted minimum mean square error (WMMSE).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable neural architecture for spatial encoding in ultra-large-scale communication systems. It will provide a new optimization framework for NTNs that explicitly accounts for global constellation state, leading to demonstrable gains in spectral efficiency and robustness. The resulting model can serve as a core 'intelligent' agent for future autonomous satellite network operation.
proposals.novelty: While graph neural networks have been explored for wireless networks, the application of graph transformers to model the explicit, physics-based long-range dependencies in a massive LEO constellation for joint beamforming and handover optimization is novel. This bridges the gap between emerging geometric deep learning techniques and the large-scale system optimization themes central to Prof. Debbah's research.

proposals.title: Meta-Reinforcement Learning with Latent Task Embeddings for Sample-Efficient Adaptation in Dynamic Satellite Networks
proposals.motivation: Future LEO satellite constellations must autonomously manage resources and control handovers in highly dynamic environments with varying orbital patterns and non-uniform user traffic. Traditional optimization and single-task RL methods are too slow to adapt online or require prohibitive amounts of new data for each novel configuration. A meta-learning framework is needed to enable rapid, sample-efficient policy adaptation across the diverse 'tasks' encountered in integrated space-air-ground networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-RL algorithm, trained on a distribution of simulated satellite network tasks, can learn a shared latent embedding space that encapsulates the core dynamics of orbital mechanics and user distributions. Conditioning a neural network policy on an embedding inferred from limited online experience will enable faster and more robust adaptation than fine-tuning from scratch or using context-agnostic policies.
proposals.method: We will develop a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) or Reptile-based framework. The 'task' is defined by parameters for satellite orbits (e.g., inclination, altitude) and ground user spatial distributions. A recurrent or transformer-based encoder network will map a short trajectory of state-action-reward tuples to a latent task embedding. This embedding will condition a policy network, implemented via hypernetwork modulation or feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM), to specialize its control actions. Training will use a curriculum of tasks generated from system models, optimizing for post-adaptation performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel meta-RL architecture for conditional policy generation in satellite networks, 2) a rigorous benchmark for adaptation speed and final performance compared to baseline RL and optimization methods, and 3) new insights into the transfer of meta-learning concepts from computational biology to complex communication systems control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit creation and use of a learned, interpretable latent task embedding for satellite network control—a concept inspired by bioinformatics—combined with a conditioning mechanism that allows a single meta-trained policy to rapidly specialize, moving beyond one-size-fits-all or slow fine-tuning approaches in existing satellite communications literature.

proposals.title: Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Fast Adaptation in Dynamic Multi-Beam Satellite Networks with Variable System Scale
proposals.motivation: Future satellite networks (e.g., LEO constellations) must serve dynamic user populations with agile, reconfigurable antenna beams, leading to frequent and unpredictable changes in system dimensionality. Conventional optimization or deep RL methods require extensive re-training or re-convergence after each topological shift, causing significant latency and service degradation in these highly dynamic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) controller, trained over a distribution of network states with varying numbers of users and active beams, can learn a prior that enables rapid online adaptation to new, unseen system scales, achieving near-optimal resource orchestration with minimal fine-tuning steps.
proposals.method: We will develop a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) framework integrated with a deep RL agent (e.g., PPO or SAC). The outer-loop meta-training will expose the agent to a wide distribution of MDPs, each defined by a specific (N_users, M_beams) configuration. The inner-loop will perform a few steps of policy gradient adaptation. The agent's policy network will output joint beamforming (precoding) vectors and user scheduling decisions, with system state incorporating channel conditions from RIS/FAS-inspired models and the current active topology.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel meta-RL framework for scalable and adaptive resource orchestration in satellite communications. 2) Demonstrated significant reduction in re-convergence time after topological changes compared to baseline RL and optimization methods. 3) A principled methodology to handle variable action and state spaces inherent to reconfigurable multi-beam systems.
proposals.novelty: While meta-RL has been explored for wireless networks, its application to the joint problem of beamforming and scheduling in satellite systems where *both* the action space (beams) and user set scale dynamically is novel. This moves beyond fixed-size optimization and single-task RL to a truly agile, system-agnostic controller.

proposals.title: Distributed and Adaptive Physics-Informed Learning for Robust Orbit Determination in Heterogeneous LEO Constellations
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of heterogeneous low-Earth orbit (LEO) constellations, comprising satellites with vastly different sensing, compute, and power capabilities, creates a critical need for resilient, in-situ collaborative state estimation. Traditional centralized orbit determination is infeasible due to latency and bandwidth constraints, while existing distributed methods often ignore the underlying physics of orbital mechanics or fail to adapt to the network's dynamic resource heterogeneity, leading to suboptimal accuracy and robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical, physics-informed neural network (PINN) architecture, where the physics-based loss functions and model components are intelligently partitioned and optimized across satellites according to their real-time resource profiles, will enable more accurate and resilient distributed orbit determination than static or physics-agnostic federated learning approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tier hierarchical PINN framework. A master model, residing on a capable node, will learn a high-fidelity surrogate of orbital mechanics. Resource-constrained satellites will host lightweight student models or learn residual terms, guided by local observations and physics-informed losses tailored to their compute budget. The distributed optimization will leverage an adaptive federated averaging scheme, inspired by rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) principles for efficient information exchange, where the aggregation weights and the complexity of shared parameters are dynamically adjusted based on link quality and node capability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel distributed optimization framework for physics-informed machine learning in dynamic networks, directly applicable to next-generation satellite communications and sensing systems. It will provide concrete algorithms for adaptive model partitioning and federated training under heterogeneous constraints, advancing the field of intelligent networked systems.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty lies in the co-design of adaptive model architecture splitting and distributed optimization for PINNs, moving beyond static federated learning to a system where the physics-based learning task itself is decomposed and allocated across a heterogeneous network based on real-time resource states.

proposals.title: Dynamic Trust and Robust Aggregation for Byzantine-Resilient Decentralized Learning in LEO Satellite Constellations
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large-scale low-Earth orbit (LEO) satellite constellations for global sensing and communication necessitates decentralized, on-board machine learning for autonomous coordination and resource management. However, the harsh space environment and potential for cyber-attacks introduce Byzantine faults and adversarial agents, which can severely degrade or disrupt conventional decentralized learning algorithms like decentralized stochastic gradient descent (D-SGD). Pre-2026, robust aggregation methods exist but often rely on strong assumptions about data distribution or require global knowledge, which is impractical for dynamic, resource-constrained satellite networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a lightweight, locally computed dynamic trust model with a robust aggregation rule will enable provable convergence to a reliable global model in a decentralized satellite network, even under a bounded fraction of Byzantine agents, using only local communications and without assuming data homogeneity.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-agent system where each satellite agent maintains a local trust score for every neighbor, updated based on the historical consistency and predictive utility of received model updates. This trust matrix will weight contributions in a robust aggregation step, such as a trimmed mean or geometric median, applied to the models received via intermittent inter-satellite links (ISLs). We will formulate the joint learning and trust update as a bi-level optimization problem, analyze its convergence using martingale and concentration inequalities, and simulate it in a digital twin of a LEO constellation with time-varying topology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practical algorithm for Byzantine-resilient decentralized learning in non-stationary, spatially distributed systems. It will deliver a theoretical convergence guarantee under dynamic trust and bounded adversaries, and a publicly available simulation framework for satellite-constellation-specific ML robustness benchmarking.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the synthesis of a *dynamic*, *data-driven trust mechanism* computed purely from local model exchange with robust aggregation, tailored for the specific temporal and connectivity constraints of satellite constellations, moving beyond static or reputation-based trust models.

proposals.title: Stigmergic Multi-Agent Learning for Satellite Constellations: A Decentralized Optimization Framework Inspired by Ant Colonies
proposals.motivation: The operation of large-scale, low-Earth-orbit satellite constellations for global coverage and low-latency communications requires robust, decentralized coordination to optimize resource allocation, routing, and sensing tasks. Centralized control is infeasible due to latency, single points of failure, and scaling issues. Existing multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) approaches for satellites often rely on complex, bandwidth-intensive global state sharing, which contradicts the constrained inter-satellite link (ISL) environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a decentralized learning framework, where satellites communicate only local success metrics as digital pheromones via ISLs, can enable the emergent optimization of global constellation performance metrics, such as total data throughput or observation coverage, without centralized control.
proposals.method: We will formulate a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) for each satellite agent. Each agent employs a local policy network, updated via a policy gradient method. The key innovation is a stigmergic communication layer: agents broadcast scalar success signals (pheromones) associated with their state-action pairs. Neighboring agents receive and aggregate these signals using an ACO-inspired evaporation and reinforcement rule, which then biases their local policy updates, creating positive feedback for collectively beneficial actions. The system will be simulated using a high-fidelity satellite network simulator.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, bio-inspired MARL algorithm specifically designed for the communication and latency constraints of satellite networks. It will provide a rigorous analysis of the convergence properties and emergent behavior of the stigmergic system, offering a new paradigm for scalable and resilient space-based network optimization.
proposals.novelty: While ACO is classic for combinatorial optimization, its direct integration as a communication and credit-assignment mechanism within a continuous policy gradient MARL framework for dynamic satellite constellations is novel. This moves beyond using ACO merely as a routing algorithm to using it as a core principle for decentralized learning agent interaction.

proposals.title: Decentralized Multi-Agent Learning for Cooperative Satellite Resource Allocation and Beamforming
proposals.motivation: Future low Earth orbit (LEO) mega-constellations require robust, real-time resource management to maximize network sum-rate and minimize interference under dynamic channel conditions. Centralized optimization is infeasible due to latency, single-point failure, and the massive scale of the system. A decentralized approach where satellites act as intelligent agents with local observations is essential for scalable and resilient satellite communications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework, where satellites cooperate via constrained local message passing, can achieve near-centralized performance in joint beamforming and power allocation while ensuring scalability and robustness to link failures.
proposals.method: We will formulate the satellite network as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP). Each satellite will be modeled as an agent implementing a deep recurrent Q-network (DRQN) or a multi-agent proximal policy optimization (MAPPO) variant to handle partial observability. Cooperation will be enforced through a novel attention-based communication mechanism, allowing agents to share compressed embeddings of their local state (e.g., interference measurements, user queue status) only with neighboring satellites, adhering to pre-2026 MARL principles for decentralized execution.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel decentralized MARL framework specifically tailored for large-scale LEO satellite networks. It will provide rigorous analysis of the trade-off between communication overhead and system performance (sum-rate, fairness) and demonstrate robustness against dynamic topology changes. The resulting protocol will be a key enabler for autonomous, intelligent satellite networks.
proposals.novelty: While MARL has been studied for terrestrial networks, its application to the unique constraints of satellite constellations—with highly dynamic 3D topologies, intermittent inter-satellite links, and the need for ultra-reliable operation—is largely unexplored. The integration of attention-based message passing for decentralized cooperation in this context is a significant departure from existing centralized or fully distributed optimization methods.

proposals.title: Decentralized Emergent Coordination for Resilient Service Migration in Ad-Hoc Drone Swarms
proposals.motivation: Future intelligent communication systems, such as drone swarms for emergency response or tactical networks, require robust service migration to maintain data flow and computational offloading. Centralized controllers, as used in prior works like GATE-HPPO, present a single point of failure and are vulnerable to jamming, partitions, and node failures in dynamic ad-hoc environments. A fully decentralized solution is critical for resilience in contested or disaster scenarios where infrastructure is absent.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid coordination model combining local swarm-preserving behavioral rules with a gossip-based distributed reinforcement learning protocol will enable emergent, robust service migration decisions, maintaining higher service continuity and swarm integrity under adversarial conditions than centralized or purely local approaches.
proposals.method: We propose a two-layer architecture. First, a local rule-based layer ensures basic swarm cohesion and connectivity using pre-2026 flocking and coverage algorithms. Second, a decentralized RL layer employs independent actors on each drone, trained via a gossip protocol where drones exchange compact policy gradient updates or value function approximations only with immediate neighbors. This leverages pre-2026 concepts of federated and multi-agent RL but constrains communication to an ad-hoc gossip substrate, avoiding any central aggregator. We will evaluate using custom simulations modeling drone mobility, service demand, and jamming attacks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, fully decentralized framework for resilient service migration in mobile ad-hoc networks, directly addressing the centralization flaw. It will provide new metrics and benchmarks for swarm resilience under attack and contribute to the theory of emergent coordination in distributed AI systems for communications.
proposals.novelty: The integration of gossip-based distributed RL with emergent local behavioral rules for a unified swarm control and service optimization task is non-obvious. It moves beyond centralized training or federated averaging to a truly decentralized, peer-to-peer learning protocol tailored for highly dynamic and adversarial aerial networks.

proposals.title: Stigmergic Service Migration: Integrating Ant Colony Optimization with a GATE-HPPO Framework for Resilient Drone Swarm Networks
proposals.motivation: Future 6G and intelligent communication systems will rely on autonomous drone swarms for dynamic service provisioning, but managing service migration in such ad-hoc, resource-constrained networks remains a critical challenge. Existing centralized or purely model-free RL approaches struggle with scalability and adaptability in highly dynamic, large-scale swarm environments.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting the GATE-HPPO (Graph Attention Network-enhanced Proximal Policy Optimization) framework with a decentralized, ACO-inspired digital pheromone mechanism will enable emergent, swarm-wide optimization of service migration paths, leading to superior resilience, faster convergence, and higher service quality compared to purely global or local policies.
proposals.method: We will design a hybrid architecture where each drone runs a local HPPO agent for migration decisions. A digital pheromone field, mapped onto the network graph, is created where successful migrations deposit pheromones proportional to QoS metrics (e.g., latency, success rate). This field undergoes time-based evaporation. The GATE module is modified to ingest both node/edge features and the local pheromone intensity as an additional environmental signal, allowing the agent's policy to integrate this swarm-wide stigmergic feedback. We will implement and test this in a simulated drone network environment, benchmarking against baseline GATE-HPPO and other MARL schemes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel hybrid AI framework that combines the strengths of centralized learning (via GATE-HPPO) and decentralized, bio-inspired swarm intelligence. It will provide a new methodology for scalable and resilient service management in mobile ad-hoc networks, advancing the field of agentic AI for future communications.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in using a biologically-inspired stigmergic signal (ACO pheromones) as a learnable input feature for a graph-attention-based deep RL agent, creating a tight feedback loop between emergent swarm behavior and individual agent policy refinement, which is unexplored for service migration.

proposals.title: Link-Aware Epidemic Gossip Learning for Resilient Federated Analytics in LEO Mega-Constellations
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large-scale low-Earth orbit (LEO) mega-constellations for global sensing and communication generates vast, distributed data. Centralized federated learning (FL) is infeasible due to the prohibitive latency and bandwidth cost of aggregating models via a terrestrial server through intermittent satellite-to-ground links. A decentralized, topology-agnostic learning framework is essential to leverage this in-situ data for real-time, constellation-wide intelligence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a decentralized learning system based on epidemic gossip protocols, enhanced with link-aware contact scheduling and robust asynchronous aggregation, will achieve convergence rates comparable to centralized FL while providing inherent resilience to the dynamic topology and link intermittency of LEO constellations.
proposals.method: We will design a system where each satellite maintains a local model updated via stochastic gradient descent on its observed data. Inspired by randomized gossip algorithms, satellites will periodically initiate pairwise averaging (consensus) with a randomly selected neighbor within contact. The protocol will be enhanced by: 1) a Link-Aware Message Scheduling (LAMS) algorithm that prioritizes contacts with higher predicted bandwidth and stability, optimizing the gossip *rate*; and 2) robust aggregation functions (e.g., incorporating momentum or gradient clipping) to tolerate delayed and out-of-order model updates. We will formulate the joint optimization of contact scheduling and consensus weights as a stochastic network problem.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully decentralized learning framework specifically tailored for mega-constellations, eliminating the central aggregator bottleneck. It will provide rigorous convergence analysis under time-varying graphs and intermittent links, and a practical protocol design validated via satellite network emulation.
proposals.novelty: While gossip algorithms are known, their integration with link-aware scheduling and robust aggregation for learning in highly dynamic LEO networks, moving from large AI models to an *agentic* distributed intelligence system, is a novel contribution to space-air-ground integrated networks.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Federated Learning for Mega-Constellations: An Immunological-Inspired Architecture for Scalable On-Orbit Graph Processing
proposals.motivation: Mega-constellations of thousands of satellites generate massive, distributed graph-structured data (e.g., network topologies, Earth observation correlations). Centralized processing is infeasible due to latency and bandwidth constraints, while standard federated learning (FL) struggles with extreme client counts and non-IID data. Pre-2026, scaling FL to such dynamic, resource-constrained systems remains a critical unsolved problem for real-time space-based analytics and network optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical FL architecture, inspired by the adaptive immune system's localized training and compact memory sharing, can achieve scalable and communication-efficient learning across mega-constellations by enforcing structured, multi-tier aggregation of distilled model knowledge.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier FL system: 1) Local clusters of satellites form 'orbital lymph nodes,' performing localized training of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on their sub-graphs using a robust optimization objective. 2) Inspired by immune memory, clusters distill updates into compact signature embeddings or pruned model differentials. 3) A ground-based or leader-satellite aggregator performs higher-tier aggregation using techniques like Federated Averaging (FedAvg) or control variates, then broadcasts a refined global model. We will analyze convergence using a temporal system model and optimize communication rates via intelligent resource-aware scheduling.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel FL framework specifically designed for ultra-large-scale satellite networks, providing formal analysis of its scalability and convergence. It will yield concrete algorithms for communication-efficient, distributed graph processing in space, directly impacting the design of future intelligent and autonomous mega-constellations.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the principled translation of immunological principles—localized adaptation and compact systemic memory—into a hierarchical FL design for space systems, moving beyond simple client-server FL to a biologically-inspired, multi-tier aggregation for extreme scalability.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Federated Learning for Joint Beamforming and Resource Optimization in Mega-LEO Constellations
proposals.motivation: The deployment of mega-constellations with thousands of low-Earth orbit (LEO) satellites promises global coverage and high-capacity 6G non-terrestrial networks (NTN). However, pre-2026, the centralized optimization of such a massive, dynamic, and spatially distributed system is computationally intractable and suffers from excessive signaling overhead and latency. A scalable, intelligent framework is urgently needed to manage interference, handovers, and resource allocation across thousands of moving nodes.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-tier hierarchical federated learning (HFL) framework, enhanced with graph neural networks (GNNs) for topology awareness, can achieve near-optimal system-wide performance for joint precoding and resource allocation in mega-constellations, while drastically reducing convergence time and communication overhead compared to centralized or flat federated approaches.
proposals.method: We propose a three-tier HFL architecture: user terminals/satellites (clients), gateway-based satellite clusters (middle aggregators), and a central network controller (global server). Each cluster employs a GNN-based local model, trained via federated averaging (FedAvg), to optimize robust precoding (e.g., RSMA-based) and sub-channel allocation within its dynamic sub-graph. The central server performs meta-aggregation of cluster models using a temporal attention mechanism to capture system-wide spatio-temporal dependencies. Optimization will leverage constrained learning via Lagrangian multipliers (LAMS) to enforce power and interference constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first scalable HFL-GNN optimization framework specifically designed for mega-LEO constellations, providing concrete algorithms and convergence guarantees. It will yield a significant reduction in required signaling and computational load for constellation-wide management, directly addressing a key bottleneck for practical 6G NTN deployment.
proposals.novelty: The integration of GNNs within a hierarchical FL structure to explicitly model the time-varying mega-constellation topology and optimize communication *rates* and robustness jointly is novel, moving beyond flat FL for static networks or centralized optimization heuristics prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Jointly Optimized Service Migration and Incremental Edge Infrastructure Placement for Geographically Constrained User Populations
proposals.motivation: The practical deployment of mobile edge computing (MEC) and non-terrestrial networks (NTN) faces a critical challenge: user populations are often fixed in constrained geographical areas (e.g., urban canyons, remote communities), while infrastructure placement and service migration are dynamic. Pre-2026 research often treats user locations as variable or optimizes migration and placement separately, leading to inefficient, non-robust blueprints for real-world, capital-constrained deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a joint stochastic optimization framework, which co-designs the dynamic service migration policy and the strategic, budget-constrained placement of new edge nodes, will yield a significantly more robust and lower-latency system blueprint than sequential or decoupled approaches under immutable user distributions.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a two-stage stochastic program. The first stage solves a constrained facility location problem (using Lagrangian methods or Benders decomposition) to place a limited budget of new nodes. The second stage models service migration as a Markov decision process (MDP), where the policy is optimized using deep reinforcement learning (DRL), specifically a multi-agent actor-critic framework, to adapt to stochastic user demand and the fixed node placements. The two stages will be iteratively solved using a primal-dual or alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) approach.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, implementable optimization framework for the co-design of infrastructure and control planes in constrained-edge networks. It will deliver novel algorithms for joint placement and migration, and a set of quantitative benchmarks showing the latency/disruption gains over decoupled baselines, directly informing cost-effective 6G and NTN deployment strategies.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the tight coupling of a *static*, budget-constrained infrastructure placement problem with a *dynamic* service migration MDP under the fundamental constraint of an immutable user geography, moving beyond traditional sequential or network-agnostic optimization.

proposals.title: Joint Optimization of LEO Constellation Sizing and Strategic Gateway Placement for Cost-Effective Global Coverage with QoS Guarantees
proposals.motivation: The deployment of massive Low Earth Orbit (LEO) constellations for global broadband represents a monumental capital expenditure, where the number of satellites is often sized for worst-case, uniformly distributed demand. Simultaneously, the placement of ground gateways, a major terrestrial cost, is typically planned for a fixed constellation. This decoupled approach leads to systemic inefficiency. In the pre-2026 push for affordable global connectivity, a holistic, system-level optimization framework is critically needed to minimize total system cost while guaranteeing end-to-end Quality of Service (QoS).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by jointly optimizing the satellite constellation parameters (e.g., number of orbital planes, satellites per plane, altitude) and the geographic placement of a limited number of intelligent gateways under a defined service migration policy, a significant reduction in the required scale of the space segment can be achieved for the same QoS, revealing a fundamental cost Pareto frontier for integrated space-terrestrial networks.
proposals.method: We will formulate a bi-level optimization problem. The upper level minimizes total cost (satellite launch + gateway deployment) subject to a minimum rate and latency guarantee. The lower level, for a given configuration, models the temporal dynamics of coverage and handovers using a graph-based system model, integrating tools from Debbah's work on 3D radiation fields and robust precoding for MIMO feeder links. We will solve this using a hybrid meta-heuristic (e.g., a Genetic Algorithm for constellation/gateway search) combined with convex optimization for the lower-level resource allocation, leveraging Large AI Model (LAM)-inspired techniques for efficient search space pruning and scenario generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel mathematical framework for the joint capital expenditure optimization of non-terrestrial networks, 2) Quantitative insights into the trade-off between space and ground infrastructure costs under statistical QoS constraints, and 3) A practical planning tool for network operators to determine minimum viable system configurations for targeted service areas.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the co-design of space and ground segments as coupled decision variables within a single optimization, moving beyond the prevailing paradigm of sizing constellations for passive, uniformly distributed users. This explicitly models how strategic, active terrestrial placement can subsidize and reduce space segment requirements.

proposals.title: Robust Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Resilient Satellite Operations Under Stochastic and Adversarial Perturbations
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of satellites and space debris, coupled with increasing geopolitical tensions, creates a critical need for autonomous, resilient satellite operations. Pre-2026, operations rely heavily on deterministic models, leaving systems vulnerable to unpredictable space weather, stochastic collision risks, and potential adversarial jamming or spoofing. A unified framework to optimize operational policies against these compound threats is urgently required for future large-scale constellations.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework, integrating physics-informed stochastic models of environmental perturbations and game-theoretic models of adversarial interference, can synthesize robust orbital and communication strategies that maintain system-level functionality and data rate guarantees under a wide spectrum of failure scenarios.
proposals.method: We propose a hierarchical MARL architecture where each satellite is an intelligent agent. The environment integrates probabilistic models for space weather (e.g., ionospheric scintillation) and debris conjunction events. Adversarial perturbations are modeled via a minimax formulation, where a learned adversarial agent attempts to degrade the system's sum-rate. We will employ a distributionally robust optimization (DRO) layer atop the MARL policy, training with the Lagrangian Alternating Multi-Agent (LAMS) method to handle the complex constraints (e.g., collision avoidance, power limits). The framework will be validated in a high-fidelity simulation built upon 3D Gaussian radiation field models for precise link analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, scalable methodology for designing resilient orbital and communication protocols. It will provide concrete metrics for system robustness and a simulation tool for stress-testing constellation architectures. The resulting policies will demonstrate superior performance in maintaining aggregate data rate and mission integrity under mixed stochastic and adversarial conditions compared to conventional predictive control.
proposals.novelty: The integration of DRO within a MARL framework specifically for space systems, combining physics-based stochastic models with active adversarial learning, moves beyond traditional robust control or isolated threat modeling. This creates a holistic agentic AI approach for space resilience.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Multi-Layer Satellite-Terrestrial Network Orchestration
ideas.description: This research fuses the multi-layer network architecture with physics-informed machine learning to create a novel orchestration framework for integrated satellite-terrestrial edge computing. The core innovation is a hybrid neural network that embeds orbital mechanics equations as hard constraints within its architecture, ensuring all predicted satellite handovers, link states, and visibility windows are physically realizable. This physics-aware model is then used to jointly optimize computation offloading, data routing, and resource allocation across the dynamic satellite constellation and the static terrestrial edge/data center layer. The result is a system that can proactively plan and execute complex workflows with guaranteed feasibility, dramatically improving latency, reliability, and resource efficiency over heuristic or purely data-driven approaches.

ideas.title: Dynamic Graph Transformers for Adaptive Multi-Satellite Networks
ideas.description: This research direction develops adaptive graph transformer networks specifically designed for satellite communication systems where the number of users and the active antenna configuration can change rapidly. The core innovation is a dynamic graph neural architecture that can add or remove nodes (representing users or antennas) and edges (representing communication links or interference) on-the-fly, while the graph transformer's attention mechanism continuously recalculates the most critical long-range dependencies across the satellite constellation. This enables real-time, context-aware beamforming and resource allocation that is both globally informed by the entire network state and immediately adaptable to topological changes, significantly outperforming static models or algorithms that lack this integrated spatial-temporal adaptability.

ideas.title: Dynamic Resource Orchestration for Multi-Layer Satellite-Terrestrial Networks with Volatile User and Antenna Populations
ideas.description: This research proposes the design of adaptive, cross-layer orchestration algorithms for integrated satellite-terrestrial networks where both the user population and the available antenna configurations (e.g., on satellites, ground stations, or user terminals) can change rapidly. The core challenge is to jointly optimize communication and computational resource allocation across the satellite constellation, terrestrial edge servers, and core data centers in real-time, under these dynamic constraints. The novel synergy lies in creating a unified control plane that treats volatile antennas and users as intrinsic system states, triggering seamless re-partitioning of workloads and spectral resources across the network's spatial and architectural layers to maintain optimal global utility and resilience.

ideas.title: Extend to multi-layer satellite-terrestrial networks
ideas.description: Generalize the model to include terrestrial edge servers and data centers for joint optimization.
ideas.sourceTitle: Graph-Aware Temporal Encoder-Based Service Migration and Resource Allocation in Satellite Networks

ideas.title: Hierarchical Federated Learning for Robust RSMA in Finite Blocklength Mega-Constellations
ideas.description: This research direction investigates a hierarchical federated learning (HFL) framework to design robust rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) precoders for finite blocklength (FBL) communications in mega-constellations comprising thousands of satellites. The core challenge is to scalably optimize precoding for stringent reliability-latency constraints (FBL) under imperfect channel state information (robust design) across an extremely large and dynamic network. The proposed HFL architecture organizes satellites into clusters with intra-cluster model aggregation, followed by inter-cluster or orbital-plane aggregation, to efficiently learn robust RSMA policies that adapt to local channel conditions and traffic demands. This synergy creates a scalable solution for joint resource and interference management in next-generation non-terrestrial networks, where centralized optimization is infeasible and standard methods lack the necessary robustness for short-packet, low-latency services.

ideas.title: Joint Reinforcement Learning-Based User Scheduling and Robust Precoding for RSMA
ideas.description: This research proposes a unified framework that jointly optimizes user scheduling and robust precoding for Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) in imperfect CSI scenarios. The core innovation is a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agent that interacts with a robust precoding solver, treating it as a dynamic environment. The agent selects users and assigns common/private streams, receives a reward based on the actual robust precoding outcome, and learns a policy that maximizes long-term system performance under uncertainty. This end-to-end approach overcomes the fundamental suboptimality of decoupled designs by ensuring the scheduler explicitly accounts for the constraints and opportunities of the robust physical-layer transmission scheme.

ideas.title: Heterogeneous Constellation-Aware Temporal Scheduling with Adaptive Attention
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel scheduling and task allocation framework for heterogeneous satellite constellations that integrates long-term temporal dependency modeling with real-time resource awareness. The core innovation is a temporal encoder with adaptive attention mechanisms that dynamically adjusts its complexity and look-back horizon based on the compute, storage, and power profile of the satellite currently being scheduled. This allows the system to capture critical long-term orbital and environmental patterns for optimal planning, while strictly respecting the instantaneous capabilities and energy budgets of each heterogeneous node. Consequently, the constellation can collectively achieve higher-fidelity, temporally-informed coordination than a homogeneous system, without overloading its less capable members.

ideas.title: Joint Optimization of Service Migration Policy and Incremental Infrastructure Placement for Constrained User Distributions
ideas.description: This research addresses the practical deployment of mobile edge computing and non-terrestrial networks by solving a joint optimization problem under real-world constraints. Given a fixed, geographically constrained distribution of ground users, the system co-designs the service migration policy and the strategic placement of a limited budget of new, deployable gateways or edge nodes. The objective is to minimize average latency or service disruption, acknowledging that most user locations are immutable. The approach combines stochastic user demand models, constrained facility location optimization, and dynamic policy adaptation to produce a robust, implementable deployment blueprint.

ideas.title: Co-modeling physical optical links with network protocol dynamics for accurate edge service prediction
ideas.description: This approach extends the graph model by integrating a lightweight, embedded discrete-event network simulator at each satellite node to model the protocol and scheduling behavior that governs inter-satellite links. Each graph edge is characterized by both its physical optical parameters and a derived service profile generated from simulated contention, handshakes, and traffic prioritization. This allows the model to predict not just theoretical capacity and latency, but the actual realized performance under specific routing and load conditions, closing the model-reality gap for network-aware path planning and resource allocation.

ideas.title: Integrate knowledge bases with LAMs for communications
ideas.description: Combine structured network knowledge with LAM reasoning for improved decision-making.
ideas.sourceTitle: From Large AI Models to Agentic AI: A Tutorial on Future Intelligent Communications

ideas.title: Meta-Learning for Adaptive Resource Orchestration in Dynamic Multi-Beam Satellite Networks
ideas.description: This research proposes a meta-reinforcement learning framework for satellite communication systems where both the number of users and the available antenna beams can change rapidly and unpredictably. The core innovation is to meta-train a controller to quickly learn optimal beamforming and user scheduling policies not just for a fixed system size, but for a distribution of possible system scales and antenna configurations. This enables the network to intelligently reconfigure its resource allocation strategy on-the-fly when users join/leave or when antennas are activated/deactivated, achieving near-optimal performance with minimal re-convergence time after each topological shift.

ideas.title: Construct large-scale communication-specific datasets
ideas.description: Build comprehensive datasets for training LAMs on network traffic, channel states, and protocol behaviors.
ideas.sourceTitle: From Large AI Models to Agentic AI: A Tutorial on Future Intelligent Communications

ideas.title: Scale up: Continent-wide multi-agent system orchestration
ideas.description: Coordinate millions of AI agents across a global 6G network for holistic optimization.
ideas.sourceTitle: From Large AI Models to Agentic AI: A Tutorial on Future Intelligent Communications

ideas.title: Cellular-Inspired Federated Learning for Mega-Constellation Graph Processing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from cellular immunology to the challenge of scaling federated learning for mega-constellations with thousands of satellites. In immunology, the adaptive immune system efficiently searches a massive antigen space through localized training in lymph nodes and systemic sharing of compact immune memory. Analogously, we propose a hierarchical federated learning architecture where satellites are organized into local clusters. Each cluster performs localized graph neural network training on its observational or network graph data. Following the immune system's principle, these clusters then exchange only distilled model updates or learned 'signature' embeddings—not raw data—with a higher-tier orbital or ground-based aggregator. This approach drastically reduces the communication burden across the constellation, enhances scalability to extreme graph sizes by partitioning the problem, and maintains robustness as the system can adapt locally to new data patterns, similar to a secondary immune response.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning with Task Embeddings for Adaptive Satellite Control
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from computational biology, where meta-learning with task embeddings accelerates the prediction of protein functions, this idea adapts the solution for fast policy adaptation in satellite networks. The core is to apply meta-reinforcement learning to learn a shared latent embedding space that encapsulates the essential features of various orbital patterns and ground user distributions. During online operation, the system uses a small amount of new experience to infer the correct task embedding for the current context. This embedding then serves as a conditioning input or a modulator for a neural network policy, allowing it to specialize its behavior rapidly and with high sample efficiency, much like predicting a protein's function from its sequence to guide engineering.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Cell Rate-Splitting for LEO Satellite Networks with Uncertain Dynamics
ideas.description: This research synthesizes robust multi-cell Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) design with models of uncertain orbital dynamics to develop a next-generation precoding and resource allocation framework for dense Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks. It treats inter-satellite and satellite-to-terrestrial interference as a dynamic, multi-cell problem where cell geometries and channel states are perturbed by stochastic orbital deviations and potential node failures. The core innovation is a two-tier robust optimization that protects against both imperfect channel state information on the ground and the macroscopic uncertainty in the satellite constellation's topology, ensuring service continuity and spectral efficiency. This leads to a communication strategy that is fundamentally co-designed with the operational realities of non-geostationary satellite systems.

ideas.title: Multi-Layer Satellite-Terrestrial Network Optimization via Metabolic Pathway Flux Analysis
ideas.description: This research seed proposes generalizing a satellite network model to include terrestrial edge servers and data centers for joint optimization. By drawing an analogy from Systems Biology, specifically Flux Balance Analysis (FBA) used to optimize flows in multi-layered metabolic networks, we adapt its core solution. The analogous FBA solution models interconnected biological pathways as a stoichiometric system and uses linear programming to find optimal metabolite flux. Adapted to the networking context, we propose modeling the satellite-terrestrial-edge-cloud continuum as a multi-layer resource transformation network. A 'network stoichiometry' matrix will encode how data and compute tasks are processed and routed between orbital and terrestrial layers. A constrained optimization framework will then be developed to solve for the optimal joint flow (data routing and task offloading decisions) that maximizes a system-wide objective (e.g., quality of service, throughput) under the distinct physical and resource constraints of each network tier.

ideas.title: Non-Markovian Mobility-Aware Low-Complexity Scheduling for RSMA
ideas.description: This research direction integrates non-Markovian models of user mobility, which capture long-term dependencies and habitual patterns, into the design of low-complexity scheduling algorithms for RSMA networks. By leveraging the predictability inherent in memory-dependent mobility, the scheduler can make informed, proactive decisions on user grouping and power allocation for common and private streams, reducing the need for frequent, reactive optimization. This approach significantly lowers computational complexity compared to full-scale instantaneous optimization while improving spectral efficiency and quality-of-service by anticipating interference patterns and channel state transitions. The result is a practical, robust RSMA framework uniquely tailored for real-world user movement, bridging the gap between accurate mobility modeling and feasible network implementation.

ideas.title: Apply Graph Transformers with Protein Fold-Style Positional Encoding for Satellite Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational biology, specifically protein structure prediction, to the problem of capturing long-range dependencies in satellite networks. In protein folding models like AlphaFold2, graph transformers use sophisticated pairwise positional encodings derived from 3D spatial relationships and evolutionary data to understand interactions between distant amino acids. Analogously, for a constellation of satellites, we propose using a graph transformer where each satellite is a node. The key adaptation is to generate dynamic, pairwise positional encodings based on the evolving 3D spatial relationships between satellites (e.g., relative distance vectors, visibility windows, and angular separations in an Earth-centered frame). These encodings are injected into the attention layers of the graph transformer, providing an inductive bias that directly represents the physical constellation geometry. This allows the model to prioritize attention between satellites that are spatially or temporally linked for tasks like collision avoidance, communication routing, or coordinated sensing, even if they are far apart in their orbital parameters, thereby better capturing the complex, long-range dependencies inherent in multi-satellite systems.

ideas.title: Multi-Timescale Hierarchical Coordination for Satellite-Terrestrial-Cloud Computing Fabrics
ideas.description: This research designs a scalable coordination framework for multi-layer satellite-terrestrial-data center networks, avoiding the pitfalls of monolithic optimization. The core innovation is a hierarchical, multi-timescale decomposition where each network layer (satellite constellation, terrestrial edge, cloud data center) performs autonomous, latency-sensitive optimization using its own specialized models and objectives. These layers are coordinated by a high-level resource broker operating on a slower timescale, which uses a market-inspired mechanism to negotiate inter-layer resource contracts and service level agreements (SLAs) based on predicted demand and supply. The resulting system ensures global efficiency and resilience while maintaining tractability, operational independence for each domain, and realistic signaling overhead.

ideas.title: Develop specialized LAMs for 6G communication tasks
ideas.description: Create LAMs specifically for network optimization, resource allocation, and signal processing in 6G systems.
ideas.sourceTitle: From Large AI Models to Agentic AI: A Tutorial on Future Intelligent Communications

ideas.title: Interference-Aligned RSMA for Proactive Service Migration in Drone Swarms
ideas.description: This research fuses interference-aligned Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) precoder design with the GATE-HPPO framework for managing service migration in mobile drone ad-hoc networks. The core innovation is to use the predictive capabilities of GATE-HPPO—which anticipates topology changes and migration needs—to inform and optimize the interference alignment strategy of the RSMA precoders in real-time. This co-design allows the swarm to not just reactively manage interference for current connections, but to proactively structure the signal space to protect the high-priority, latency-sensitive data flows generated during service migration events. Consequently, the fusion achieves robust, high-SNR communication for control and migrated service data simultaneously, significantly improving the reliability and efficiency of dynamic compute offloading across the drone network.

ideas.title: Joint Constellation Sizing and Strategic User Placement for Guaranteed QoS
ideas.description: This research investigates the integrated optimization problem of determining the minimum viable satellite constellation while also prescribing optimal geographic placement strategies for ground users or gateways, all under a fixed service migration policy. It moves beyond sizing a constellation for a static demand or placing users for a fixed network, instead solving for the most cost-effective system configuration where both space and ground elements are decision variables constrained by end-to-end service guarantees. The core novelty is a bi-level or joint optimization model that reveals how strategic terrestrial placement can reduce the required scale (and thus cost) of the space segment, or vice-versa, identifying Pareto-optimal frontiers. This provides a foundational tool for network planners and service providers to make holistic capital expenditure decisions for next-generation non-terrestrial networks.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning Robust SIC Adaptation for Dynamic Non-Terrestrial Networks
ideas.description: This research fuses imperfect SIC modeling with meta-reinforcement learning to develop a robust and adaptive transmission policy for non-terrestrial networks (e.g., LEO satellite constellations). The core idea is to meta-train a policy over a wide distribution of simulated SIC imperfection models (e.g., residual interference levels, error propagation characteristics) and dynamic network topologies. This equips the policy with a prior for quickly inferring the current, real-world SIC imperfection profile from limited pilot observations and adapting its resource allocation (e.g., power, decoding order) accordingly. The result is a system that maintains high spectral efficiency and fairness despite unknown and potentially changing receiver hardware impairments and user distributions, without needing lengthy re-training for each new scenario.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Riemannian Optimization for Constant-Modulus Precoding in Mega-Constellations
ideas.description: This research direction develops a hierarchical optimization framework that solves the massive-scale constant-modulus precoding problem for mega-constellations by structuring the computation across the satellite network's topology. It leverages Riemannian optimization on the complex Stiefel manifold to inherently satisfy per-antenna constant-modulus constraints at each satellite node. A federated or hierarchical learning strategy is then used to coordinate these local manifold optimizations across clusters and the entire constellation, allowing for scalable, distributed interference management. This synergy creates a system capable of achieving near-optimal global precoding for thousands of satellites while respecting both the rigid hardware constraints and the practical limitations of inter-satellite communication.

ideas.title: Dual: Use network to optimize AI training distribution
ideas.description: Leverage the 6G communication fabric to efficiently distribute LAM training tasks across nodes.
ideas.sourceTitle: From Large AI Models to Agentic AI: A Tutorial on Future Intelligent Communications

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Robust RSMA for Finite Blocklength with Model-Agnostic Reliability Guarantees
ideas.description: This research proposes a meta-reinforcement learning framework to design robust Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) strategies for ultra-reliable low-latency communications (URLLC) in the finite blocklength regime. Instead of relying on precise but often inaccurate analytical models for channel uncertainty and short-packet error rates, the system learns a robust precoding and rate-splitting policy from interaction with the communication environment via limited pilots and feedback. The core innovation is a meta-training procedure that exposes the learning agent to a wide distribution of potential uncertainty models and blocklength conditions, enabling it to quickly adapt and provide reliable performance guarantees in novel, unseen scenarios with minimal online fine-tuning. This results in a model-agnostic scheme that fundamentally overcomes the fragility of model-based robust designs in the face of real-world imperfections.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Distributed Orbit Determination in Heterogeneous Constellations
ideas.description: This research proposes a hierarchical, physics-informed neural network (PINN) architecture designed for a heterogeneous satellite constellation. The system intelligently partitions the physics-based loss functions and model components across satellites based on their individual compute, storage, and power profiles, with resource-constrained nodes handling simpler surrogate models or residuals. This enables in-situ, collaborative learning where the constellation as a whole enforces orbital mechanics constraints on shared observation data, improving prediction accuracy and resilience. The key innovation is a distributed optimization scheme that adapts the fidelity and distribution of the physics-informed learning tasks to the dynamic and heterogeneous capabilities of the orbital network.

ideas.title: Meta-Testing: A Meta-Learning Framework for Real-World Satellite Network Robustness
ideas.description: This research proposes Meta-Testing, a framework that integrates real-world orbital traces and user demand data directly into a meta-reinforcement learning (Meta-RL) training loop. Instead of validating a single static policy, the system is meta-trained on a diverse, historical dataset of real constellation states and demand fluctuations, learning a prior that captures the underlying dynamics of satellite networks. This enables the core GATE-HPPO agent to perform few-shot adaptation when deployed, quickly converging to an effective policy for a new satellite deployment pattern or a sudden change in geographic user demand, thereby providing robust and efficient satellite network management in dynamic real-world conditions.

ideas.title: Adaptive Task Offloading Inspired by Ecological Resource Partitioning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a satellite constellation's computational layer should present a homogeneous resource pool. Drawing an analogy from ecology's principle of resource partitioning, where diverse species coexist by specializing in different niches within a shared habitat, we propose to model the constellation as a computational ecosystem. The analogous solution—niche differentiation to reduce competition and increase total system efficiency—is adapted into a scheduling and offloading framework that actively partitions and routes tasks based on the heterogeneous compute, storage, and power profiles of the satellites. Lightweight 'forager' satellites handle sensing and relay, medium-capability nodes perform intermediate aggregation, and high-capability 'specialist' hubs execute complex analytics, creating a dynamically balanced and resilient processing chain that maximizes the utility of the inherent hardware diversity.

ideas.title: Energy-Optimal Neural Surrogates for Large-Scale, Resource-Abundant Systems
ideas.description: This research investigates the use of deep neural networks as surrogate models for the control and coordination of large-scale, resource-abundant systems, such as massive MIMO arrays, distributed sensor networks, or data center clusters. By inverting the scarcity assumption, the core optimization objective shifts from traditional metrics like spectral efficiency to minimizing total energy consumption per task. The neural network is trained to approximate a complex, joint optimization that discovers and maps the system's high-dimensional state to energy-optimal configurations, which may involve intelligently activating or deactivating subsets of the abundant resources. This enables real-time, sustainable management of systems at a scale and efficiency unattainable by conventional optimization or neural methods designed for constrained scenarios.

ideas.title: Satellite Swarm Learning via Ant Colony Optimization Principles
ideas.description: This idea applies the principles of Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) from swarm intelligence to multi-agent decentralized learning for satellite constellations. The analogous solution from entomology involves ants using decentralized, stigmergic communication (pheromone trails) to solve complex problems like pathfinding with only local environmental interactions. Adapted to the research context, each satellite in a constellation functions as an autonomous learning agent. It broadcasts local success metrics—conceptual 'digital pheromones'—regarding its actions (e.g., data routing decisions, target observation success) to its immediate neighbors via inter-satellite links. A decentralized reinforcement learning or policy gradient algorithm is formulated where each agent adjusts its local policy parameters based on the weighted aggregation of pheromone signals it receives, creating positive feedback for successful collective behaviors. This enables the satellite swarm to cooperatively optimize global objectives—such as Earth observation coverage or communication latency—through purely local information exchange and without any central controller, mimicking the emergent intelligence of an ant colony.

ideas.title: Resilient Decentralized Learning for Mega-Constellations via Epidemic Gossip Protocols
ideas.description: This research designs a learning system for satellite mega-constellations that operates without any central or hierarchical aggregator, eliminating the communication bottleneck. It employs randomized gossip protocols where each satellite periodically exchanges and averages its model parameters with a randomly selected neighbor within contact, allowing updates to diffuse epidemically across the network. The protocol is enhanced with link-aware scheduling to prioritize high-bandwidth contacts and robust aggregation functions that tolerate asynchronous, delayed, and lost messages. This results in a constellation that collectively learns from globally distributed data with inherent resilience to node failures, dynamic topology changes, and intermittent connectivity.

ideas.title: Resilient Satellite Operations Under Stochastic and Adversarial Perturbations
ideas.description: This research develops a robust decision-making framework for satellite operations that explicitly models and mitigates the most critical non-deterministic threats. It integrates probabilistic models of space weather, debris collision risk, and potential adversarial interference into a dynamic simulation and planning tool. The framework enables operators to evaluate the resilience of different orbital strategies and contingency plans against a wide spectrum of plausible failure scenarios. The output is a validated methodology for designing orbital architectures and operational protocols that maintain functionality despite unpredictable perturbations and hostile actions.

ideas.title: Apply multi-agent decentralized learning
ideas.description: Formulate the problem for decentralized execution where satellites cooperate with local information.
ideas.sourceTitle: Graph-Aware Temporal Encoder-Based Service Migration and Resource Allocation in Satellite Networks

ideas.title: Implement multi-agent collaboration for network management
ideas.description: Develop frameworks for multiple AI agents to collaboratively plan and control network resources.
ideas.sourceTitle: From Large AI Models to Agentic AI: A Tutorial on Future Intelligent Communications

ideas.title: Applying Ant Colony Optimization Principles to GATE-HPPO for Drone Swarm Service Migration
ideas.description: This research expands the original idea of applying the GATE-HPPO framework to manage service migration in mobile ad-hoc drone networks by drawing an analogy from Swarm Intelligence, specifically Ant Colony Optimization (ACO). The analogous solution involves simulating ant foraging behavior, where pheromone trails guide the colony to efficient paths. We adapt this by integrating a digital pheromone mechanism into the GATE-HPPO framework. Drones executing successful service migrations deposit virtual pheromones along the network path used, with pheromone intensity correlating to service quality metrics (e.g., throughput, success). These pheromones evaporate over time, ensuring adaptability. The HPPO agent's policy network is then augmented to consider these localized, swarm-generated pheromone signals in addition to its own state and global objectives. This hybrid approach enables the drone network to exhibit emergent, decentralized optimization of service migration routes, improving resilience and efficiency in dynamic, scaled-down swarm environments by leveraging stigmergic communication principles observed in biological systems.

ideas.title: Adapting Neurovascular Coupling Models for Dynamic Inter-Satellite Link Features
ideas.description: This research expands the graph model for satellite networks by integrating inter-satellite optical link capacity and latency as dynamic edge features. The analogy is drawn from computational neuroscience, specifically neurovascular coupling models (e.g., the Balloon-Windkessel model), which solve the analogous problem of modeling how blood flow dynamics (capacity/latency) change in response to neural activity (data demand). The analogous solution uses a dynamic system approach where vascular edge properties are functions of local signals and physical constraints. This is adapted by treating each inter-satellite link as a dynamic system whose key features—capacity and latency—are governed by coupled equations. These equations update based on a local demand signal (traffic load, priority) and the link's physical state (pointing accuracy, range, Doppler), allowing the network graph to model the temporal evolution of link performance under varying operational conditions, much like modeling blood flow response in a neural network.

ideas.sourceTitle: From Large AI Models to Agentic AI: A Tutorial on Future Intelligent Communications
