ideas.description: Extend the graph model to include inter-satellite link capacity and latency as dynamic edge features.
ideas.sourceTitle: Graph-Aware Temporal Encoder-Based Service Migration and Resource Allocation in Satellite Networks

ideas.title: Challenge homogeneous agent design for all tasks
ideas.description: Create heterogeneous agent architectures specialized for distinct network control and user-plane functions.
ideas.sourceTitle: From Large AI Models to Agentic AI: A Tutorial on Future Intelligent Communications

ideas.title: Robust User Placement under Stochastic Orbital Dynamics and Service Migration
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework for the robust placement of ground users or gateways in a satellite network, where the orbital dynamics are non-deterministic. It inverts the traditional problem by treating user placement as a decision variable that must be optimized in conjunction with a given service migration policy to hedge against satellite perturbations and potential failures. The core synergy is a two-stage stochastic or robust optimization model that finds ground station locations which minimize expected service disruption, considering the probabilistic evolution of the constellation's state. This enables the joint assessment of infrastructure strategy and operational resilience, a capability absent when considering orbital uncertainty or placement optimization in isolation.

ideas.title: Invert the assumption of stable network semantics
ideas.description: Design LAMs that adapt to rapidly changing protocol semantics and user intent.
ideas.sourceTitle: From Large AI Models to Agentic AI: A Tutorial on Future Intelligent Communications

ideas.title: Byzantine-Resilient Multi-Agent Decentralized Learning with Dynamic Trust for Satellite Constellations
ideas.description: This research designs a decentralized learning system for satellite constellations that is robust to adversarial and faulty agents. It combines robust aggregation algorithms with a dynamic, locally computed trust model, where each agent assesses the credibility of its peers based on the consistency and performance of their shared model updates. The system ensures convergence to a reliable global policy even when a bounded subset of agents are malicious or experiencing failures, maintaining cooperative objectives using strictly local information and communication.

ideas.title: Resilient Service Migration via Decentralized Emergent Coordination in Ad-Hoc Drone Swarms
ideas.description: This research designs a fully decentralized system for managing service migration in mobile ad-hoc drone networks, directly addressing the centralization flaw of the original GATE-HPPO approach. It employs a hybrid coordination model combining local behavioral rules for swarm integrity with a gossip-based distributed reinforcement learning protocol, where drones exchange compact policy updates only with immediate neighbors. This enables robust, emergent decision-making for service placement and migration without any central controller, ensuring continued operation despite network partitions, node failures, or jamming. The system is evaluated on metrics of service continuity, swarm resilience under attack, and communication efficiency compared to centralized baselines.

ideas.title: Scale up to mega-constellations with thousands of satellites
ideas.description: Develop hierarchical or federated learning versions to handle extreme graph sizes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Graph-Aware Temporal Encoder-Based Service Migration and Resource Allocation in Satellite Networks

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Hop NOMA in LEO Constellations with Imperfect SIC and Dynamic Optical Inter-Satellite Links
ideas.description: This research fuses dynamic inter-satellite optical link modeling with imperfect successive interference cancellation (SIC) to create a robust framework for non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) in multi-hop low-Earth orbit (LEO) satellite constellations. The core idea is to model the graph of the constellation with time-varying edge features (capacity, latency) for optical links and use this to constrain and inform the physical-layer design against imperfect SIC. The synergy arises by making user grouping, power allocation, and multi-hop routing decisions that are jointly robust to both volatile inter-satellite link states and receiver-side SIC imperfections, which is a problem neither domain addresses in isolation. The outcome is a cross-layer optimization strategy that ensures end-to-end quality of service by dynamically balancing the data flow through the constellation's physical network against the residual interference limits imposed by practical user receivers.

ideas.title: Challenge the need for massive centralized training
ideas.description: Explore federated or decentralized learning to build LAMs without pooling all communication data centrally.
ideas.sourceTitle: From Large AI Models to Agentic AI: A Tutorial on Future Intelligent Communications

ideas.title: Adaptive Task Scheduling for RSMA using Multi-Armed Bandit Algorithms
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from operations research and reinforcement learning—specifically, low-complexity multi-armed bandit (MAB) algorithms like UCB or Thompson Sampling—to design efficient user scheduling for RSMA. The analogous problem is the sequential resource allocation or task scheduling under uncertainty, where MAB algorithms excel by balancing exploration of new options with exploitation of known good ones. The adapted idea treats the combinatorial user scheduling space in RSMA as a set of arms with initially unknown performance rewards (e.g., spectral efficiency or robustness when paired with the proposed precoding scheme). An online MAB-based scheduler would select user groups for transmission, receive noisy feedback on the outcome (e.g., from the robust precoder's performance), and gradually learn an optimal scheduling policy. This approach avoids the high computational complexity of exhaustive search or traditional optimization, providing a low-complexity, adaptive scheduling algorithm compatible with dynamic wireless environments and the existing robust precoding framework.

ideas.title: Adaptive Resource Allocation via Swarm Intelligence Algorithms
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from computational biology and swarm intelligence to the challenge of rapidly changing user counts and antenna configurations in wireless systems. The analogous problem of resource allocation and pathfinding in dynamic, decentralized biological systems (like ant colonies) is solved through stigmergy and local agent interactions, leading to robust, emergent adaptability. The adapted idea proposes designing communication protocols and signal processing algorithms where user equipment and access points function as simple agents. These agents use local channel measurements and broadcast lightweight, informative signals (analogous to pheromones) to enable the network to self-organize. This approach allows for real-time adaptation of pilot assignment, beamforming, and antenna clustering without heavy central computation, making the system scalable and robust to rapid changes in the number of active users or available antenna elements.

ideas.title: Provably Stable Adaptive Resource Allocation for Systems with Rapidly Switching Dimensions
ideas.description: This research designs and analyzes adaptive algorithms for wireless and distributed systems where the number of users or antennas can change arbitrarily fast. The core innovation is a dual-layer control structure: a fast, heuristic adaptation layer is governed by a slower, stability-verifying meta-layer that uses switched Lyapunov functions and online learning theory to certify actions. This ensures all reconfigurations maintain bounded regret and avoid instability, providing rigorous performance guarantees absent in purely heuristic adaptive methods. The outcome is a framework for dynamic systems that is both highly responsive and mathematically robust.

ideas.title: Online Meta-Learning for Model-Agnostic Robust RSMA Precoding under Dynamic CSI Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research proposes a meta-learning framework for robust RSMA precoding that dynamically adapts to the true, unknown characteristics of CSI imperfection at both transmitter and receivers. The system uses an online learning loop where short sequences of pilot and data signals are used to infer the latent statistical properties of the current CSI error environment. A meta-trained neural network quickly adjusts the robust precoding policy (e.g., common and private stream precoders, power allocation) based on this inferred model, moving beyond a single, pre-defined error assumption. This results in a model-agnostic approach that maintains high spectral efficiency and fairness across non-stationary channels and varying quality of channel estimation.

ideas.title: Stochastic Optimization for CSI Distribution via Portfolio Theory Adaptation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the stochastic optimization approach from financial portfolio theory to the problem of robust wireless design under statistical CSI. In portfolio optimization, asset returns are stochastic, and the goal is to find a robust allocation that maximizes expected return for a given risk level, typically solved via sample-average approximation with historical scenarios. Analogously, in wireless communications, the CSI across multiple users, antennas, and frequencies can be viewed as a portfolio of stochastic 'channel returns.' The adapted research idea employs stochastic gradient methods or SAA to design transmit strategies (e.g., beamforming vectors, power allocation) that optimize an expected utility metric (like sum spectral efficiency or proportional fairness) subject to constraints on outage probability or quality-of-service, using only the probability distribution of the CSI rather than its instantaneous realizations. This enables robust system design that performs well on average over the channel distribution, reducing sensitivity to rapid CSI variations and feedback overhead.

ideas.sourceTitle: Robust Precoding Designs of RSMA for Multiuser MIMO Systems

ideas.title: Dual: Constrained Optimization of Satellite Constellations via Ecological Niche Packing
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the niche packing problem from community ecology to the domain of satellite constellation scaling. In ecology, the goal is to find the minimum set of species and their trait distributions required to fully utilize available resources while ensuring ecosystem function. Analogously, here we seek the minimum constellation size and orbital configuration (shells, planes, phasing) required to fully satisfy a set of target Quality of Service (QoS) metrics—such as coverage percentage, revisit time, and latency—treating the service volume and user demand as the 'resource' to be exploited. By modeling orbital parameters as trait axes and QoS requirements as stability constraints, we develop a constrained optimization framework that 'packs' satellites into the orbital space with maximal efficiency, determining the absolute lower bound for constellation scale under guaranteed performance.

ideas.title: Extend model to multi-user MIMO scenarios

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Multi-Agent Learning for Decentralized Satellite Swarm Control
ideas.description: This research develops a decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning framework where each satellite agent employs a physics-informed neural network (PINN) as its policy or critic. The PINN architecture hard-constrains action outputs or value estimates to satisfy known orbital dynamics (e.g., via Hamiltonian or Lagrangian layers), ensuring all proposed maneuvers are physically realizable. Agents learn cooperative tasks, like formation reconfiguration or debris avoidance, using only local sensor data, while the embedded physics acts as a universal coordination signal, preventing the swarm from learning physically impossible or highly inefficient strategies that pure black-box learning might discover.

ideas.title: Adversarially Learned Interference Models for Tractably Robust System Design
ideas.description: This approach replaces intractable analytical robust optimization with a tractable, adversarial learning procedure to handle complex, non-Gaussian, and correlated interference. A deep generative model is trained to capture the high-dimensional statistics and correlation structures of interference from historical or simulated data. This generator acts as an adaptive 'adversary' within a minimax optimization, producing challenging interference realizations against which a neural network controller optimizes the communication or sensing system's parameters. The resulting design is robust to interference drawn from the learned, realistic distribution, avoiding the excessive conservatism of classical models while remaining computationally feasible.

ideas.title: Robust Penalty-Based Riemannian Optimization with Learned Impairment Models for Practical Constant-Modulus Precoding
ideas.description: This method addresses the fragility of pure manifold optimization by formulating the precoding problem as an unconstrained optimization on a surrogate manifold with a composite objective. The objective combines the communication metric (e.g., sum-rate) with adaptive penalty terms that softly enforce constant-modulus and orthogonality, where the penalty weights are dynamically tuned based on a learned model of hardware-specific impairments. The impairment model, built from offline calibration data, informs the adaptation of both the penalty landscape and the Riemannian metric itself, making the optimization robust to real-world non-idealities. This results in practically feasible precoders that maintain near-constant modulus performance while being resilient to model mismatches and hardware distortions.

ideas.title: Neural Network as a Surrogate Solver for Alternating Optimization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the surrogate modeling approach from Computational Fluid Dynamics to reduce the complexity of an alternating optimization algorithm for real-time implementation. In CFD, neural networks are trained to approximate the results of expensive numerical simulations, enabling real-time prediction. Similarly, we propose to train a deep neural network offline on a comprehensive dataset generated by the proposed alternating optimization algorithm. This network will learn to directly map system states and parameters to near-optimal solutions. For deployment, the lightweight neural surrogate replaces the iterative optimization loop, providing a massive reduction in computational complexity and enabling real-time performance while maintaining high solution fidelity.

ideas.title: Optimal Sensor Placement for Inverse Estimation in Structural Health Monitoring
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the inverse sensor placement problem from Structural Health Monitoring to optimize ground user or gateway placement in a network with a fixed service migration policy. In SHM, optimal sensor locations are chosen to maximize the information (e.g., via D-optimal design) for estimating unknown parameters, given a fixed structural model and analysis algorithm. Analogously, here we treat user/gateway positions as 'sensors' providing input to the fixed migration policy. The adapted idea is to formulate and solve an optimal experimental design problem where the objective is to place users to maximize a metric of system observability or to minimize the uncertainty in key performance indicators (like end-to-end latency or migration triggers), thereby ensuring the fixed policy operates on the most informative data possible for optimal overall system performance.

ideas.title: Regime-Aware Adaptive Optimization Under Dynamic Resource Landscapes
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for systems that must operate across both resource-scarce and resource-abundant regimes. Instead of assuming a fixed state of abundance, it models resource flux as a dynamic variable influenced by technical, economic, and environmental factors. The core innovation is an optimization engine that can dynamically reconfigure its primary objective and constraints based on the detected or forecasted resource regime. This enables a single system to be robust and high-performing whether resources are constrained, abundant, or in transition, moving beyond a brittle single-regime assumption.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning for Distributionally Robust Optimization with Online Adaptation to Non-Stationary CSI
ideas.description: This system addresses the critical flaw of distributional shift in stochastic optimization for CSI by unifying meta-learning and online robust adaptation. A meta-learning phase pre-trains a model on a wide spectrum of historical CSI distribution scenarios, learning a parameter initialization that can adapt quickly to new statistical conditions. During online operation, a distributionally robust stochastic optimizer continuously processes incoming CSI samples, using discrepancy measures to identify shifts and adjust its risk-sensitive objective accordingly. This ensures the communication system design remains robust and near-optimal even as the underlying channel statistics evolve, overcoming the brittleness of purely offline stochastic methods.

ideas.title: Adversarial Multi-Scenario Stress Testing for LEO Network Resource Management
ideas.description: This research rigorously evaluates the GATE-HPPO framework by subjecting it to a comprehensive suite of adversarial and uncertain operational scenarios beyond historical data. A simulation testbed is developed that merges real satellite constellation ephemerides and demand patterns with synthetically generated high-stress events, including random satellite failures, emergency collision avoidance procedures, dynamic spectrum contention, and rapidly shifting geopolitical demand hotspots. The framework's performance is measured by its resilience and decision-making quality across this entire scenario distribution, ensuring robustness against the unpredictable challenges of real-world mega-constellation operations. This approach provides a more credible assessment of readiness for deployment than validation on a single historical trace.

ideas.title: Robust Orbital Control via Ecological Resilience Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to managing complex ecosystems under unpredictable disturbances—ecological resilience theory—to the challenge of orbital dynamics. The analogous problem in ecology involves maintaining ecosystem services despite stochastic events like fires or species invasions, which is solved by modeling system regimes, thresholds, and early-warning signals to guide adaptive management within a safe operating space. The adapted idea applies this to orbital mechanics by relaxing the assumption of perfectly known and deterministic dynamics. It proposes modeling orbital perturbations and potential satellite failures not as precise events to be predicted, but as potential regime shifts in a dynamical system. The research would develop a framework to identify probabilistic, data-driven early-warning indicators (e.g., from telemetry and tracking residuals) that signal an increased risk of leaving a 'safe' operational regime. The decision-making framework would then prioritize robust, adaptive control actions that maintain the system's resilience—such as strategic fuel reserves, redundant maneuver options, or constellation reconfigurations—over actions that are optimal only under perfectly known conditions. This creates a robust decision-making paradigm for satellite operations that is explicitly designed for uncertainty and partial system failures.

ideas.title: Physics-Structured Sparse Attention for Orbital Dynamics
ideas.description: This approach enhances the temporal encoder by embedding a differentiable orbital mechanics model as a structural prior, explicitly capturing known long-term gravitational dependencies. A sparse, gated attention mechanism is then applied only to the residuals and shorter-term interactions, focusing its capacity on learning deviations from the physical model. This hybrid design ensures computational efficiency and strong generalization by grounding the model in known physics, while the data-driven component adapts to complex, unmodeled dynamics. The system is trained end-to-end, allowing the attention mechanism to correct and complement the physical kernel.

ideas.title: Model user mobility using path integral methods from polymer physics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the Markovian assumption in user mobility modeling by drawing an analogy from polymer physics. In that field, the non-Markovian, memory-dependent conformation of polymer chains is successfully modeled using path integral methods, which sum over all possible configurations weighted by an energy functional. Adapted to mobility research, we propose defining a 'mobility action' functional that encodes constraints like geographical feasibility, temporal routines, and semantic preferences (e.g., 'going to a gym after work'). The probability of a user's observed trajectory is then computed via a path integral over possible routes, effectively capturing complex, long-range dependencies and habitual patterns that simple Markov models miss. This provides a foundational framework for more accurate mobility prediction and simulation.

ideas.title: ResilientDual: Co-optimizing for Minimum Size and Guaranteed Robustness in Satellite Constellations
ideas.description: This research develops a methodology to find the smallest satellite constellation that not only meets target Quality of Service (QoS) metrics under ideal conditions but also guarantees those metrics remain satisfied under a defined set of realistic failures and dynamic perturbations. It explicitly models stochastic events such as random satellite failures, increased atmospheric drag, and mandatory collision avoidance maneuvers. The output is a constellation configuration and size that is provably robust within specified probabilistic bounds, providing a practical design that balances cost-efficiency with operational resilience.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Folding Optimization on Energy Landscapes to Constant-Modulus Precoding
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using Riemannian manifold optimization to solve precoding problems with constant-modulus constraints, which define the complex Stiefel manifold. By analogy, the field of computational biochemistry solves the analogous problem of protein folding—optimizing a molecular structure on a constrained conformational manifold (governed by bond geometry) to minimize an energy function. The analogous solution employs hybrid algorithms that combine stochastic global search on the manifold with local Riemannian gradient refinement to handle the non-convex landscape. Adapted to wireless communications, this leads to a research idea for a hybrid precoding optimizer. The method would perform a global stochastic search (e.g., a manifold-aware genetic algorithm) over the Stiefel manifold to skirt local optima in the spectral efficiency or error rate objective, followed by a precise, fast convergence using a tailored Riemannian gradient-based method. This hybrid approach aims to provide more robust and higher-performance solutions for constant-modulus precoding in MIMO systems compared to purely local manifold optimization methods.

ideas.title: Apply Traffic Flow Optimization Principles to RSMA Precoder Design
ideas.description: This idea integrates interference alignment principles to structure RSMA precoders for high SNR by drawing an analogy from transportation engineering. The analogous problem is optimizing traffic flow at a complex intersection, solved by aligning vehicle streams into distinct, non-conflicting phases using signal timing and lane markings. The adapted research idea applies this principle by designing RSMA precoders that actively align multi-user interference into a minimized subspace at each receiver, analogous to dedicating a traffic phase for cross-flow. This structured alignment clears the remaining signal dimensions (like open lanes) for the efficient, high-SNR delivery of private and common messages, thereby maximizing the sum-rate and managing interference in dense networks.

ideas.title: Temporally-Aware Robust Precoding Under Dynamic Power Amplifier Non-Linearity
ideas.description: This research direction fuses robust precoding under PA non-linearity with temporal attention mechanisms to create a predictive, efficiency-aware transmission framework for non-terrestrial networks (e.g., LEO satellite constellations). The core innovation is a temporally-robust precoding optimization that incorporates a dynamic, state-aware model of PA non-linearity, where the model parameters (e.g., AM/AM, AM/PM characteristics, operating point) are predicted over time by an encoder capturing long-term orbital and operational patterns. This allows the precoder to not only be robust to current non-linear distortion but to also schedule power and signal dimensions proactively, minimizing distortion peaks and improving overall power efficiency across the satellite's orbital cycle. The result is a significant enhancement in both spectral efficiency and PA energy utilization compared to systems treating non-linearity as a static or short-term impairment.

ideas.title: Optimizing for Energy Efficiency in Resource-Abundant Computing via Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This research seed inverts the traditional computing assumption of resource scarcity. By drawing an analogy from ecology—specifically the study of ecological succession in resource-abundant environments—we adapt the solution of shifting optimization objectives from survival to efficiency metrics like energy flow. The adapted idea applies this to computing: under conditions of abundant computational resources (CPU, memory, storage, bandwidth), we should redesign algorithms and system architectures to explicitly optimize for minimizing total energy consumption or joules per operation, rather than for resource conservation. This paradigm shift, mirroring the transition from r-strategist to K-strategist in ecology, could lead to novel computing principles where energy, not silicon or time, becomes the primary limiting factor to optimize for in abundant regimes.

ideas.title: Adaptive Spectral Memory for Orbital Pattern Encoding
ideas.description: This idea enhances the temporal encoder by incorporating mechanisms to explicitly capture long-term periodic orbital patterns, drawing an analogy from signal processing for physiological time series. In fields like cardiology, adaptive filter banks and time-frequency analyses are used to isolate and track long-term quasi-periodic components (e.g., circadian rhythms) within noisy data, often coupled with memory models to capture dependencies. Adapted to the orbital context, we propose integrating a learnable spectral decomposition layer into the encoder architecture. This layer would decompose the temporal input into a set of features aligned with fundamental and harmonic orbital periods. An attention mechanism would then be applied over this structured, multi-scale temporal representation, enabling the model to identify and leverage long-range phase relationships, resonant behaviors, and slowly modulating periodic patterns critical for accurate long-term prediction and understanding of orbital dynamics.

ideas.title: Online Learning and Adaptive Compensation for Non-Stationary Hardware Impairments in Robust RSMA
ideas.description: This research proposes a closed-loop robust RSMA design that dynamically learns and tracks real-time transceiver hardware impairments. The system employs an online impairment estimation engine, using in-band pilots and low-overhead sensing, to construct a time-varying impairment profile. This profile is integrated into a min-max or chance-constrained robust precoding optimization, which is solved adaptively to ensure performance guarantees under both imperfect channel state information and the evolving, non-ideal hardware behavior. The result is a self-correcting physical layer design that maintains robustness against the double uncertainty of the wireless channel and the hardware's own dynamic imperfections.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmacokinetic Robust Control to RSMA for Finite Blocklength
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to investigate robust Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) design for the finite blocklength (FBL) regime by drawing an analogy from robust control in pharmacokinetics. In drug delivery, robust adaptive control algorithms (e.g., Model Predictive Control with uncertainty sets) are used to design dosing regimens that maintain drug concentration within a narrow therapeutic window for all patients, despite significant inter-patient variability and finite, noisy observations. Analogously, for short-packet communications, we must design RSMA precoders that satisfy strict reliability (error probability) and latency (blocklength) constraints for all users, despite imperfect Channel State Information at the Transmitter (CSIT) modeled within a bounded uncertainty set. The adapted idea is to formulate a robust optimization problem where the precoding strategy ensures the actual FBL error probability remains below a target threshold for the worst-case CSIT realization within its uncertainty set, effectively creating a 'robust FBL performance region'. This transforms the pharmacokinetic goal of robust therapeutic efficacy into a communication goal of robust ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) using RSMA.

ideas.title: Online Learning and Adaptive Control for Unknown and Dynamic SIC Imperfections
ideas.description: This research designs a framework that treats SIC imperfection parameters as unknown and time-varying states to be estimated, rather than as a static model to be robustified against. The system uses a combination of short pilot sequences and decoding success/failure feedback from users to construct real-time, user-specific estimates of residual interference power and error propagation statistics. These estimates then dynamically inform a robust non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) scheduler that jointly adapts power allocation, user pairing, and decoding orders to maximize a throughput metric that accounts for estimation uncertainty. This closed-loop approach ensures resilience against the practical scenario where SIC capabilities are neither perfectly known nor stationary.

ideas.title: Manifold-Optimized Sparse Satellite Precoding for Minimal Constellations
ideas.description: This research develops a novel optimization framework to determine the absolute minimum number of satellites required for a LEO constellation by simultaneously solving for orbital parameters and multi-satellite cooperative precoding. The core innovation is formulating the joint problem on a product manifold combining the complex Stiefel manifold (for constant-modulus phased-array precoding constraints) and a manifold representing satellite orbital states. By solving this unified Riemannian optimization, the method finds constellation configurations where the sparse, physically constrained satellite array can still synthesize the necessary beamforming patterns to meet ground-user QoS targets, fundamentally linking information-theoretic capacity with astrodynamics for minimal system design.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Neural Networks via Hamiltonian Symmetry Embedding
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational chemistry, where physics-informed neural networks are designed to inherently respect the Hamiltonian structure of molecular systems, ensuring conservation laws are obeyed exactly. The analogous problem of predicting complex dynamical trajectories while preserving physical invariants is solved by architectures that output a scalar Hamiltonian function, from which derivatives generate the dynamics. Adapted to orbital mechanics, the neural network would be tasked with learning a Hamiltonian function H(q,p) from observational data. The predicted state derivatives (dq/dt, dp/dt) are then computed via Hamilton's equations (∂H/∂p, -∂H/∂q), hard-coding the symplectic structure of orbital mechanics into the network's forward pass. This guarantees that predictions conserve the Hamiltonian (energy) and other symmetries, leading to more physically plausible and stable long-term trajectory forecasts than a standard PINN with soft constraint penalties.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Meta-Learning for Stable, Real-Time Alternating Optimization
ideas.description: This redesigned approach embeds a deep learning model inside a safeguarded alternating optimization loop to accelerate convergence while ensuring stability. The neural network predicts a high-quality initialization and search direction, but its output is projected onto a feasible set defined by physical and safety constraints before each iteration. A meta-learning outer loop continuously tunes the network's parameters based on real-time data about the optimization's convergence rate and constraint satisfaction, enabling adaptive improvement without violating operational safety guarantees.

ideas.title: Robust Signal Decoding via Error-Correcting SIC with Biological Proofreading Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts the concept of kinetic proofreading from molecular biology to robustify design against imperfect Successive Interference Cancellation (SIC) at user receivers. In biological systems (e.g., tRNA selection in ribosomes), high fidelity is achieved despite noisy components through multi-step verification processes that discard erroneous intermediates. Analogously, we propose to model the SIC process not as a deterministic cascade but as a sequence of probabilistic decoding steps, each with an embedded 'verification checkpoint' based on estimated signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) or symbol confidence metrics. The receiver design will incorporate mechanisms to backtrack, re-weight, or even skip a cancellation stage if the confidence in the decoded symbol is below an adaptive threshold, thereby localizing errors and preventing their propagation through the cancellation chain. This approach robustifies system performance against imperfect channel estimation, quantization errors, and hardware impairments that lead to residual interference.

ideas.title: Adaptive Surrogate Selection for Majorization-Minimization in Non-Convex Sum-Rate Maximization
ideas.description: This idea enhances the standard MM framework by automating the critical step of surrogate function construction. It designs a meta-algorithm that, at each iteration, evaluates a set of candidate majorization strategies based on their theoretical tightness and empirical performance from recent steps. The system then adaptively selects or blends surrogates to optimize the convergence rate and solution quality. This results in a robust, self-tuning optimization procedure that systematically handles the non-convex sum-rate problem while mitigating the primary risk of poor surrogate choice inherent in vanilla MM.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Signal Processing for Robust Design Under Correlated Interference
ideas.description: This idea extends robust design to non-Gaussian or spatially correlated interference scenarios by drawing an analogy from geophysical signal processing. In seismology, techniques like f-k filtering and Karhunen-Loève transforms are used to separate desired signals from structured, correlated 'ground roll' noise by exploiting the noise's spatial covariance. Adapted to robust design, this involves modeling the interference with a structured covariance kernel and reformulating the robust optimization problem in a domain transformed by the eigenbasis of this covariance. This transformation decorrelates the interference, allowing the design constraints to be enforced in a more efficient, less conservative manner tailored to the specific correlation structure, rather than using generic worst-case bounds designed for independent or Gaussian disturbances.

ideas.title: Robust Precoding with PA Non-linearity via Control-Theoretic Disturbance Modeling
ideas.description: This research expands the original idea by incorporating practical power amplifier (PA) non-linearity into robust precoding design via an analogy from robust control theory. In control systems, non-linear actuator saturation is successfully handled by modeling it as a bounded, state-dependent disturbance, allowing for stability-guaranteeing designs using convex optimization techniques like LMIs. We adapt this solution by formally modeling the PA's non-linear distortion (e.g., from a Rapp or Saleh model) as a bounded, signal-dependent additive disturbance to the precoded signal. The robust precoding optimization is then reformulated to guarantee worst-case system performance (e.g., ensuring QoS constraints or maximizing a robust sum-rate metric) over all possible distortion realizations within that bounded set, transforming a complex non-linear hardware constraint into a tractable robust convex optimization problem.
